But God, in His love, sent His Son Jesus Christ—fully God and fully man—who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as our substitute bearing God's wrath for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation and assurance of eternal life—paradise with God—are not earned by works but received as a free gift by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus declared, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me" (John 14:6). All who repent of sin, trust in His atoning death and resurrection, and confess Him as Lord receive forgiveness, the Holy Spirit as a seal of assurance, and the promise of paradise forever (Romans 10:9; Ephesians 1:13-14; John 3:16).

You can turn to God right now: repent from sin, trust Jesus Christ alone as your Savior and Lord who died and rose for you, and receive this certain hope. To begin, read the Gospel of John prayerfully, asking God to reveal truth, and connect with a Bible-believing church near you for fellowship and baptism as a believer. What stands out to you in Jesus' claims, or what questions do they raise?
What is the spiritual gift of miracles?

The spiritual gift of miracles is the supernatural ability given by the Holy Spirit to certain believers to perform extraordinary acts that visibly demonstrate God's power, such as signs and wonders beyond natural means. This gift is listed among the spiritual gifts in 1 Corinthians 12:10 ("to another the working of miracles") and 1 Corinthians 12:28 ("workers of miracles"), often alongside healings and prophecy, with the purpose of authenticating the apostolic message and glorifying God, as seen in Hebrews 2:4 where God bore witness "by signs, wonders and various miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to His will."

Within historic Christianity, faithful believers hold differing views on whether this and other miraculous gifts (sometimes called "sign gifts") continue today. The cessationist position—aligned with the Reformed Baptist tradition—holds that these extraordinary gifts were given primarily to authenticate the apostles and the New Testament Scriptures, ceasing after the apostolic era (1 Corinthians 13:8-10; see also the 1689 London Baptist Confession's emphasis on the sufficiency of Scripture). Others, including cautious continuationists, believe such gifts may still operate today under strict biblical guidelines for edification and order (1 Corinthians 14:26-33). Regardless of one's view, all spiritual gifts are for building up the church and pointing to Christ, distributed sovereignly by the Spirit (1 Corinthians 12:11). If you're exploring your own gifts, prayerfully study 1 Corinthians 12 alongside Romans 12 and Ephesians 4, and discuss with mature believers in your local church.
What does it mean that God is merciful?

God's mercy means that He compassionately withholds the punishment we deserve for our sin and instead extends kindness, forgiveness, and help to those who do not merit it. This flows from His holy character, as He declares Himself "The LORD, the LORD, merciful and gracious God, slow to anger, and abounding in loving devotion and faithfulness" (Exodus 34:6).

Scripture vividly portrays this mercy: David praises God for not dealing with us according to our sins or repaying us according to our iniquities, but removing our transgressions as far as the east is from the west (Psalm 103:8-12). In the New Testament, Paul explains that God, being rich in mercy because of His great love for us, made us alive with Christ even when we were dead in our trespasses (Ephesians 2:4-5).

This truth anchors the gospel, for Christ's penal substitutionary death on the cross satisfies God's justice while displaying His mercy toward believers. Today, receive this mercy by confessing your sin to Him in prayer and trusting Christ's finished work, then meditate on Psalm 103 daily to stir gratitude and obedience.
Who is the head of the church, biblically speaking?

Biblically speaking, Jesus Christ is the head of the church. As the Apostle Paul declares, "For the husband is the head of the wife as Christ is the head of the church, His body, of which He is the Savior" (Ephesians 5:23), and "He is also the head of the body, the church; He is the beginning and the firstborn from among the dead, so that in all things He may have preeminence" (Colossians 1:18).

This truth underscores Christ's sovereign lordship and authority over every aspect of church life, from worship to mission, calling believers to submit joyfully to His guidance through His Word and the leading of the Holy Spirit. In practice, it means church leaders serve under His headship, not as ultimate rulers, fostering unity and humility among God's people.
Who is the ancient serpent of Revelation 12:9?

The ancient serpent of Revelation 12:9 is Satan, also called the devil, who leads the whole world astray and was hurled down to the earth with his angels. This identification echoes Genesis 3:1-15, where the serpent deceived Eve in the Garden of Eden, fulfilling God's curse that he would bruise the heel of the woman's offspring (the promised Messiah) while that offspring would crush his head—a prophecy ultimately realized in Christ's victory on the cross and resurrection (Colossians 2:15; Hebrews 2:14). Revelation 20:2 further confirms this as "that ancient serpent, who is the devil and Satan," binding him for a thousand years, underscoring his ultimate defeat by the sovereign God.
Book of 1 John - Bible Survey

The First Epistle of John, written by the Apostle John around AD 85-95 to believers in Asia Minor, proclaims the reality of eternal life through fellowship with the Father and Son, countering false teachers who denied Christ's true incarnation by emphasizing that Jesus came "in the flesh" (1 John 4:2-3, Berean Standard Bible).

John structures the letter around three tests of genuine faith—**doctrinal** (confession of Christ as incarnate Son of God, 4:1-6; 5:1), **moral** (obedience to God's commands, 2:3-6; 3:22-24), and **relational** (love for brothers, 3:10-18; 4:7-21)—to provide assurance of salvation for true believers while warning against self-deception. Key verses include God's nature as light (1 John 1:5), Christ's propitiation for sins (2:2), and overcoming the world by faith (5:4-5). This epistle boldly assures that "these things [are] written... so that you may know that you have eternal life" (5:13), urging believers today to walk in the light through confession, obedience, and love as evidences of abiding in Christ. For deeper study, read it alongside John's Gospel and meditate daily on one chapter, applying its tests to your own heart for growing confidence in Christ.
What happens at the final judgment?

At the final judgment, Jesus Christ will return personally and bodily as King and Judge, raising all people from the dead for accountability before God's throne, where every person—believer and unbeliever alike—will be judged according to their works as evidence of their faith or rebellion (Revelation 20:11-15; 2 Corinthians 5:10; Matthew 25:31-46).

The Bible teaches that God "has set a day in which He purposes to judge the inhabited earth in righteousness by a man whom He has appointed" (Acts 17:31, Berean Standard Bible). For those justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ's atoning death and resurrection, this judgment brings eternal reward and vindication, confirming their place in the new heavens and new earth (Romans 8:1; Revelation 21:1-4). For those who reject Christ, it means eternal separation from God in the lake of fire, as all sin receives its just penalty apart from the Savior's substitutionary sacrifice (Hebrews 9:27; Revelation 20:15).

This truth underscores the urgency of the Gospel: turn from sin today and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died for sinners and rose victorious, securing forgiveness and eternal life for all who believe (John 5:24; Romans 10:9). If you're pondering your own standing before God, what step of faith might you take next?
In what ways is becoming a Christian becoming an entirely new man / woman?

Becoming a Christian means being born again through regeneration by the Holy Spirit, so that if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come (2 Corinthians 5:17). This is not mere moral improvement but a profound, supernatural transformation rooted in union with Christ's death and resurrection, where God makes us entirely new from the inside out.

Here are the key ways Scripture describes this newness:

1. **A new heart and spirit**: God removes our heart of stone—rebellious and dead in sin—and gives us a heart of flesh, responsive to Him, along with His Spirit within us to empower obedience (Ezekiel 36:26-27). No longer slaves to sin, we walk in newness of life (Romans 6:4).

2. **A new standing before God**: Through justification by faith alone, our sins are forgiven, Christ's righteousness is imputed to us, and we are adopted as children of God. The old guilty self dies with Christ, and we rise justified, holy in His sight (Romans 6:6-11; 2 Corinthians 5:21).

3. **A new nature and identity**: The old self, corrupted by sin, is crucified; we put off the old man and put on the new self, created to be like God in righteousness and holiness (Ephesians 4:22-24; Colossians 3:9-10). Our core identity shifts from sinner to saint, from enemy to friend of God (Romans 5:10).

4. **New desires and direction**: The Holy Spirit renews our minds, giving us a hunger for God's Word, hatred of sin, and love for what is good. We bear fruit of the Spirit—love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control—instead of fleshly works (Galatians 5:22-23; Romans 12:2).

5. **Progressive renewal in practice**: While positionally new at conversion, we grow in progressive sanctification, becoming more like Christ through the means of grace—Scripture, prayer, and fellowship—until glorification (Philippians 1:6; 2 Corinthians 3:18).

This newness comes solely by grace through faith in Christ's atoning death and bodily resurrection, not human effort (Ephesians 2:8-9). If you sense the Spirit drawing you, trust in Jesus today: repent of sin and believe the Gospel for this new life.

To experience this more deeply, begin by reading Romans 6 daily this week, confessing specific sins to God, and asking a trusted believer to pray with you and study it together—James 5:16 reminds us the prayer of a righteous person is powerful and effective.
What are the Pauline epistles?

The Pauline epistles are the 13 letters in the New Testament written by the Apostle Paul to churches and individuals, namely Romans, 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 Thessalonians, 2 Thessalonians, 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon.

These epistles form nearly half of the New Testament and deliver core Christian doctrine, such as justification by faith alone (Romans 3:21-28), instructions for church unity and discipline (1 Corinthians 1:10; 5:1-13), and guidance for godly living amid persecution (Philippians 1:27-30). As part of Scripture's inerrant authority (2 Timothy 3:16-17), they equip believers today for sound teaching and perseverance in faith.
How can I be sure I am praying according to the will of God?

Praying with confidence according to God's will begins with aligning your requests with His revealed will in Scripture, as the Bible assures us, "This is the confidence we have in our approach to God: that if we ask anything according to His will, He hears us" (1 John 5:14, Berean Standard Bible). God's will is perfectly expressed in His Word, which teaches us His priorities—such as seeking His kingdom first (Matthew 6:33), pursuing holiness (1 Thessalonians 4:3), and submitting all things to His sovereignty (James 4:15).

Here are practical ways to grow in this assurance:

1. **Immerse yourself in Scripture daily.** The Bible is God's clear revelation of His moral and redemptive will. As you read, pray through passages like the Psalms, where David models honest cries submitted to God, or the Lord's Prayer, which Jesus taught as a perfect pattern: "Your kingdom come, Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven" (Matthew 6:10). This transforms vague prayers into ones rooted in truth.

2. **Pray in the Spirit with dependence.** The Holy Spirit helps us pray according to God's will, even when words fail (Romans 8:26-27). Cultivate a habit of pausing to listen, confessing sin that hinders fellowship (Psalm 66:18), and yielding specific requests with, "Not my will, but Yours be done," as Jesus did in Gethsemane (Luke 22:42).

3. **Seek wise counsel and examine your heart.** Share burdens with mature believers or elders, as iron sharpens iron (Proverbs 27:17), and test motives against James 4:3—do you ask from selfish ambition? Over time, answered prayers and inner peace confirm alignment (Colossians 3:15).

You may not always know God's perfect, sovereign will in every circumstance—that mystery belongs to Him (Deuteronomy 29:29)—but faithfulness in the means of grace builds trust. Start today with a simple step: set aside 15 minutes to pray slowly through Psalm 23, personalizing it and ending with submission to His will. God delights in your sincere pursuit of Him, and He promises to draw near as you do (James 4:8). Keep pressing on, dear friend; He is faithful to guide you.
What does the Bible say about busyness / being too busy?

The Bible portrays busyness as a potential snare when it crowds out devotion to God, rest, and relationships, yet it commends diligent work as stewardship of time. In Luke 10:38-42, Jesus gently rebukes Martha for being "distracted by all her preparations" while praising Mary for choosing "the good portion" of sitting at His feet, teaching that anxious busyness can hinder communion with Christ.

Scripture repeatedly calls for balance: "Be still, and know that I am God" (Psalm 46:10), urging trust over frantic striving; and the Sabbath command in Exodus 20:8-11 reminds us to cease work weekly, modeling God's rest after creation. Proverbs warns against sloth (Proverbs 6:6-11) but also against toil without purpose, as in Ecclesiastes 2:22-23, where endless labor under the sun yields vanity without God. Ephesians 5:15-16 exhorts believers to "make the most of the time, because the days are evil," prioritizing kingdom pursuits like prayer, Scripture, and loving others over mere productivity.

Practically, busyness often stems from misplaced priorities, neglecting the means of grace or family provision (1 Timothy 5:8). Take one actionable step this week: prayerfully review your schedule against Matthew 6:33—"seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness"—and carve out undistracted time daily for Bible reading and prayer, perhaps starting with 15 minutes before distractions begin.
What are the seven baptisms mentioned in the Bible, and what do they mean?

The Bible does not explicitly list or enumerate "seven baptisms," but some Christian teachers, particularly in dispensational traditions, categorize seven distinct uses of baptism (Greek: baptizō, meaning to immerse or dip) based on scriptural contexts, distinguishing between literal water rites, symbolic immersions, and spiritual realities. These highlight God's progressive revelation and Christ's fulfillment of Old Testament shadows. Here's a concise overview of the common seven, with their biblical basis and significance:

1. **John's baptism of repentance** (Matthew 3:1-6; Mark 1:4-5; Luke 3:3, 16): A water immersion symbolizing repentance from sin and preparation for the Messiah, administered by John the Baptist to Israel. It pointed forward to Christ but required re-baptism for some after Pentecost (Acts 19:1-5), as it was not in Jesus' name.

2. **Jesus' baptism** (Matthew 3:13-17; Mark 1:9-11; Luke 3:21-22): Christ's personal water baptism by John, not for repentance (as He was sinless) but for identification with sinners, fulfillment of righteousness, and inauguration of His ministry, marked by the Trinity's affirmation.

3. **Baptism of suffering** (Matthew 20:22-23; Mark 10:38-39; Luke 12:50): Jesus' metaphorical "baptism" referring to His impending death on the cross and cup of wrath, later echoed for disciples like James and John, symbolizing immersion into trials and sacrificial obedience.

4. **Baptism of the Holy Spirit** (Matthew 3:11; Mark 1:8; Luke 3:16; John 1:33; Acts 1:5; 11:16): Promised by John and Jesus, fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2:1-4), this is God's sovereign act immersing believers into Christ and uniting the church, distinct from water baptism and often linked to empowerment for witness.

5. **Water baptism in Jesus' name** (Matthew 28:19-20; Mark 16:16; Acts 2:38, 41; 8:12, 36-38; 10:47-48): The believer's baptism commanded by Christ, by immersion as a public profession of faith, repentance, and union with His death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4), practiced by New Testament churches as an ordinance of the new covenant.

6. **Baptism into one body** (1 Corinthians 12:13; cf. Romans 6:3; Galatians 3:27): A spiritual baptism by the Holy Spirit at conversion, immersing every believer—Jew or Gentile, slave or free—into the mystical body of Christ, effecting unity and the indwelling of the Spirit, not a separate human act.

7. **Baptism of fire** (Matthew 3:11-12; Luke 3:16-17): John's warning of eschatological judgment, where Christ immerses the unrepentant in purifying fire of wrath and eternal separation, contrasted with the Spirit's baptism for believers, underscoring God's holiness and the need for salvation.

These categories remind us that baptism ultimately signifies union with Christ (Romans 6:3-5), but Reformed Baptist theology emphasizes one primary ordinance for believers today—immersion upon profession of faith—as a sign of the new birth already accomplished by Spirit baptism at conversion. For deeper study, meditate on Acts 2:38 and consult your local church elders for personal application.
What do you think about the Bible codes? Is there any validity to them?

Bible codes, also known as equidistant letter sequences (ELS), claim to find hidden prophetic messages by selecting letters at fixed intervals in the Hebrew text of Scripture, such as predictions of modern events. There is no validity to them as a legitimate method of biblical interpretation or prophecy.

Many are drawn to these codes because they seem to offer mysterious insights into history or the future, tapping into our fascination with patterns. However, extensive statistical analysis shows they produce similar "predictions" in any large text, like the novel *Moby Dick* or Hebrew newspapers, revealing them as artifacts of chance rather than divine encoding. More importantly, Scripture itself emphasizes clarity and accessibility in God's communication: "Write the vision; make it plain on tablets, so that a runner may read it" (Habakkuk 2:2, Berean Standard Bible). The Bible declares its own sufficiency for doctrine, reproof, correction, and training in righteousness through its plain reading (2 Timothy 3:16-17), not hidden ciphers that require computers or selective skipping of letters.

This approach risks distracting from the gospel's clear message of Christ's atoning death and resurrection. Instead, immerse yourself in the straightforward study of God's Word—perhaps starting with a chapter from the Gospel of John each day—and let the Holy Spirit illuminate its truths for your life. What passage has been speaking to you lately?
How can I see the hand of God moving in my life?

Seeing the hand of God in your life begins with recognizing His sovereign providence—His wise and purposeful governance over all things for the good of those who love Him, as promised in Romans 8:28: "And we know that in all things God works for the good of those who love Him, who have been called according to His purpose."

Here are practical ways to cultivate eyes to perceive it:

First, immerse yourself daily in Scripture, where God reveals His character and past faithfulness. Start with Psalms, like Psalm 37:4-5: "Delight yourself in the Lord, and He will give you the desires of your heart. Commit your way to the Lord; trust in Him, and He will do it." Read a chapter each morning, noting verses that speak to your circumstances, and ask, "Lord, how are You at work here?"

Second, pray with expectancy and gratitude, journaling specific requests and later answers. Proverbs 3:5-6 instructs, "Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and lean not on your own understanding; in all your ways acknowledge Him, and He will make your paths straight." Over time, patterns emerge—unexpected provisions, inner peace amid trials, or growth in sanctification—that bear His signature.

Third, reflect on your life through a gospel lens: changed desires, fruit of the Spirit like patience in hardship (Galatians 5:22-23), or doors opened for ministry. Share observations with mature believers in your local church for confirmation, as iron sharpens iron (Proverbs 27:17).

A concrete next step: Tonight, spend 10 minutes reviewing the past week. Write three instances of provision or guidance, thanking God aloud. Repeat daily for a month, and watch how He unveils His hand more clearly.

God is faithfully at work, even when veiled, refining you as gold (1 Peter 1:6-7). Press on with hope—He who began this good work in you will carry it to completion (Philippians 1:6). If doubts linger, pray directly to your Father, confessing your need for clearer sight, and seek prayer from your church elders.
Are we to love the sinner but hate the sin?

Yes, the principle of loving the sinner while hating the sin faithfully reflects Scripture's call to extend God's love to people while upholding His holy standard against wrongdoing. God Himself demonstrates this perfectly: "But God proves His love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8, Berean Standard Bible), even as He is "of purer eyes than to behold evil" and "cannot look upon wickedness" (Habakkuk 1:13). We are commanded to "hate evil" and "love your neighbor as yourself" (Psalm 97:10; Matthew 22:39), rescuing others "with fear, hating even the garment defiled by the flesh" (Jude 1:23).

This balance means treating people with compassion, patience, and gospel hope—speaking truth in love (Ephesians 4:15)—without affirming or excusing sin, which harms both the sinner and those around them. For a practical next step, meditate on Psalm 139:23-24 this week, asking God to search your heart for any sin you tolerate, and pray for wisdom to love others redemptively as He loves you.
Is the Buddhist concept of Zen compatible with the Christian faith?

No, the Buddhist concept of Zen is not compatible with the Christian faith, as they fundamentally contradict each other on the nature of God, humanity, salvation, and reality itself.

You may wonder if Zen's emphasis on meditation and mindfulness could simply enhance Christian prayer or peace, since both traditions value inner stillness and transcendence. That's an understandable appeal in our busy world, and Christians do pursue quiet reflection, as Jesus modeled by withdrawing to pray (Luke 5:16). However, Zen Buddhism, rooted in Mahayana traditions, seeks enlightenment through self-realization and emptying the mind of dualistic thoughts to realize one's inherent Buddha-nature—without a personal Creator God, objective sin, or need for atonement. This stands in direct opposition to biblical Christianity, which teaches one eternal God existing as Trinity (Father, Son, Holy Spirit; Matthew 28:19); humanity's separation from Him due to sin (Romans 3:23); and reconciliation solely through Jesus Christ's incarnation, penal substitutionary death on the cross, burial, and bodily resurrection, received by grace alone through faith alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Scripture warns against blending incompatible worldviews, as "what fellowship does light have with darkness?" (2 Corinthians 6:14). True peace comes not from self-emptying but from knowing the Prince of Peace through repentance and trust in Him (Isaiah 9:6; John 14:27).

If you're exploring this, I encourage reading John 1:1-14 this week, praying for God to reveal Jesus as the Word made flesh. What draws you to Zen, and how might Christ's exclusive claim as the way, truth, and life (John 14:6) speak to that? Turn from any other path and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for your forgiveness and new life—He invites you today.
What is the Wailing Wall / Western Wall?

The Western Wall, also called the Wailing Wall, is a 57-meter-long exposed section of an ancient limestone retaining wall in Jerusalem's Old City, forming the western boundary of the Temple Mount platform built by Herod the Great around 19 BC to support the expanded Second Jewish Temple. This sacred site for Jews remains standing after the Romans destroyed the Temple in AD 70, serving as the closest accessible point to the former location of the Holy of Holies where God's presence dwelt.

Biblically, the Temple Mount was the heart of Israelite worship, as described in 2 Chronicles 5:7 where the Ark of the Covenant was placed there, and Jesus Himself taught and cleansed the Temple courts during His ministry (John 2:13-16; Luke 21:37). Today, it draws pilgrims who pray and insert written petitions into its cracks, lamenting the Temple's loss while awaiting future restoration promised in passages like Ezekiel 40-48.
What is Taizé worship, and is it biblical?

Taizé worship is a style of Christian liturgy developed by the ecumenical Taizé Community in Burgundy, France, founded in 1940 by Brother Roger Schultz. It emphasizes simple, repetitive chants (often short biblical phrases set to music), extended silence for meditation, candlelight, and a contemplative atmosphere aimed at fostering inner peace and unity across denominations.

Whether it is fully biblical depends on how one evaluates worship practices against Scripture, a tertiary matter where faithful Christians hold differing views under Christian liberty (Romans 14:1-6). The Bible calls believers to worship "in spirit and truth" (John 4:23-24), including speaking to one another with "psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs" (Ephesians 5:19; Colossians 3:16), and meditating deeply on God's Word day and night (Psalm 1:2). Proponents appreciate Taizé's repetition as echoing the Psalms' refrains and aiding heartfelt focus on Christ, while critics note Jesus' warning against "vain repetitions" in prayer (Matthew 6:7) and prefer worship strictly regulated by explicit biblical commands (as in the regulative principle drawn from passages like Leviticus 10:1-3 and Deuteronomy 12:32). Examine any practice prayerfully through Scripture, ensuring it exalts Christ alone, and discuss with mature believers in your local church for wise discernment.
Does the Old Testament truly predict a second advent of the Messiah?

Yes, the Old Testament truly predicts a second advent of the Messiah, portraying Him not only as a suffering servant in His first coming but also as a conquering King who returns in glory to establish everlasting dominion and judge the nations.

Several passages point to this future return. For instance, Daniel 7:13-14 describes "one like a son of man" coming with the clouds of heaven to receive an indestructible kingdom from the Ancient of Days, a vision Jesus applied to Himself (as in Matthew 24:30). Zechariah 14:3-5 depicts the Lord going out to fight against the nations, with His feet standing on the Mount of Olives, splitting it in two. Isaiah 63:1-6 paints a vivid picture of the Messiah returning from Bozrah "mighty to save," His garments stained with the blood of His enemies, executing vengeance. These prophecies, alongside others like Psalm 110:1 (the Lord at God's right hand until His enemies are made a footstool) and Micah 5:2-4 (the ruler from Bethlehem whose greatness reaches the ends of the earth), reveal a Messiah who triumphs fully after initial rejection.

The New Testament confirms this pattern, with Jesus Himself teaching that He must first suffer and then return in power (Luke 24:25-27; Acts 3:18-21). As a shepherd to your soul, I encourage meditating on these texts prayerfully, perhaps starting with Daniel 7 this week, to behold Christ's promised victory and live in hopeful readiness.
Contemporary Christian music - is it honoring to God? Should it be used in church services?

Contemporary Christian music (CCM), like any worship music, can honor God when its lyrics faithfully proclaim biblical truth, exalt Christ, and are sung from the heart with reverence (Ephesians 5:19; Colossians 3:16). However, this is a tertiary matter of worship style where faithful Christians across denominations sincerely disagree, as Scripture provides principles for worship music but no explicit commands on musical genre, instrumentation, or tempo—leaving room for interpretive emphasis and Christian liberty (Romans 14:1-6).

Two major historic Christian views include:
- **Traditionalist perspective** (common among Reformed and some Baptist churches): Prioritizes the regulative principle of worship, favoring theologically dense hymns (e.g., those by Watts or Wesley) with simple melodies that promote congregational edification and avoid emotional manipulation or cultural trends that might distract from God's Word (1 Corinthians 14:26, 40). Concerns with some CCM include shallow lyrics or performance-oriented styles that hinder corporate participation.
- **Contemporary perspective** (prevalent in evangelical and charismatic circles): Emphasizes cultural relevance and accessibility, using modern rhythms and language to engage younger believers and unreached people, provided lyrics align with sound doctrine (Psalm 96:1-3). Proponents argue it fulfills the command to "sing a new song" if it builds up the body without compromising truth.

As a shepherding principle, evaluate any music by these tests: Does it teach sound doctrine, edify the church, and direct hearts to God rather than performers (Hebrews 13:15)? A practical next step is to discuss with your church elders, reviewing specific songs against Scripture together, and prioritize whatever most unites your congregation in worshiping "in spirit and truth" (John 4:24).
What does it mean to test the spirits?

To test the spirits means to carefully discern whether a teaching, prophecy, or spiritual influence comes from God or from deceptive sources, as the Apostle John commands: "Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God. For many false prophets have gone out into the world" (1 John 4:1, Berean Standard Bible).

The primary biblical test is Christological: every spirit that confesses Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God, while any spirit that denies this—rejecting Christ's incarnation, deity, and humanity—is not from God but is the spirit of the antichrist (1 John 4:2-3). This discernment guards believers against false doctrines that undermine the Gospel's core truth of the hypostatic union, where Jesus is fully God and fully man.

In practice, apply this by measuring all spiritual claims against the full counsel of Scripture, asking: Does it affirm Christ's atoning death and resurrection as the only way to God? For example, when encountering a new teaching or "revelation," prayerfully compare it to passages like 1 John 4:1-6 or Galatians 1:8-9, and discuss it with mature believers in your local church for accountability.
Why should I get married?

Marriage is a profound gift from God, designed from creation as a covenant union between one man and one woman for companionship, mutual help, and reflecting Christ's love for His church (Genesis 2:18; "It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make a helper suitable for him"; Ephesians 5:31-32).

Scripture highlights several God-honoring reasons to pursue marriage if it aligns with your calling:
- **Deep companionship and partnership**: God created us for relational intimacy, where spouses strengthen one another in life's joys and trials (Genesis 2:24; "That is why a man leaves his father and mother and is united to his wife, and they become one flesh"; Ecclesiastes 4:9-12).
- **Sexual fulfillment and purity**: Marriage provides a covenant context for sexual union, guarding against immorality (1 Corinthians 7:2-5; Hebrews 13:4).
- **Raising godly children**: It offers a stable home to nurture the next generation in faith (Malachi 2:15; Psalm 127:3-5).
- **Mutual sanctification**: Spouses grow in Christlikeness through selfless love, forgiveness, and service (Ephesians 5:22-33).

That said, marriage is not required for a flourishing life in Christ—Paul celebrates singleness as a devoted calling that frees many for undivided service to the Lord (1 Corinthians 7:7-8, 32-35). The key question is God's will for *you*: Are you prepared to love sacrificially as Christ loves the church?

A practical next step: Spend time this week prayerfully studying Genesis 2, Ephesians 5, and 1 Corinthians 7, then discuss your readiness with a trusted pastor or mature believers in a local church—they can offer personalized wisdom grounded in Scripture. What aspects of marriage most draw you right now?
Is Sunday the Christian Sabbath?

No, Sunday is not universally regarded as the "Christian Sabbath" in orthodox Christianity, though faithful believers hold differing views on its observance as a non-essential matter where Scripture allows for liberty in conscience (Romans 14:5-6).

Two major historic Christian perspectives exist. Some, including Reformed Baptists and Presbyterians following a covenant theology of continuity, view the Lord's Day (Sunday) as the Christian Sabbath—a day set apart for worship, rest from ordinary labor, and delight in God, fulfilling the moral principle of the fourth commandment in a new covenant context (Exodus 20:8-11; Isaiah 58:13-14; based on the 1689 London Baptist Confession, chapter 22). They commemorate Christ's resurrection on the first day of the week (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2; Revelation 1:10) while prohibiting unnecessary work, much like the Old Testament Sabbath pointed forward to rest in Christ (Hebrews 4:9-10).

Others, including many Baptists, Lutherans, and broader evangelicals, distinguish the Lord's Day from the Old Testament Sabbath, seeing it primarily as a joyful time for corporate worship and fellowship to celebrate the resurrection without imposing strict Sabbath regulations, as the ceremonial aspects of the Mosaic law have been fulfilled in Christ (Colossians 2:16-17).

Whichever view aligns with your conscience after prayerful study of Scripture, prioritize gathering with your local church on the Lord's Day for preaching, prayer, and the Lord's Supper. What aspects of this observance are you pondering in your own walk?
What does it mean that those who humble themselves will be exalted (Matthew 23:12)?

Jesus teaches in Matthew 23:12 that true greatness in God's kingdom follows the principle of divine reversal: self-exaltation leads to humiliation by God, while genuine humility before Him results in exaltation by God. This comes amid His rebuke of the Pharisees' hypocritical pride and love of status (Matthew 23:1-11), contrasting their outward show with the inward reality God values.

Biblically, humility means acknowledging our dependence on God, lowering ourselves as Christ did in His incarnation and obedience (Philippians 2:5-8), rather than seeking human applause. God promises to lift up the humble, as echoed in James 4:10—"Humble yourselves before the Lord, and He will exalt you"—and 1 Peter 5:6—"Humble yourselves, therefore, under God's mighty hand, so that He may lift you up in due time."

Practically, this calls us to serve others quietly, repent of pride in prayer, and trust God's timing over self-promotion. A next step: today, identify one area of personal pride and confess it to God, asking for grace to serve someone without recognition.
How should poetry in the Bible be interpreted?

Biblical poetry, which comprises about one-third of the Old Testament (including Psalms, Proverbs, Job, Song of Solomon, and large sections of the prophets), should be interpreted by recognizing its distinctive literary features—such as parallelism, vivid imagery, metaphor, and emotive language—while discerning its intended literal meaning within its historical, literary, and canonical context to convey timeless spiritual truths.

Central to Hebrew poetry is parallelism, where lines relate to one another for emphasis or contrast: synonymous parallelism repeats an idea (as in Psalm 1:1, "Blessed is the man who does not walk in the counsel of the wicked, nor stand in the way of sinners, nor sit in the seat of scoffers"), antithetic parallelism contrasts ideas (Proverbs 10:1, "A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish son grief to his mother"), and synthetic parallelism builds progressively (Psalm 19:1, "The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies proclaim the work of His hands"). Imagery like personification (Psalm 98:8, "Let the rivers clap their hands; let the mountains sing together for joy") or hyperbole (Psalm 6:6, "I flood my bed with tears; I drench my couch with my weeping") heightens emotional and theological depth without requiring wooden literalism—rivers do not literally applaud, but all creation testifies to God's praiseworthiness.

Approach poetry prayerfully: read aloud to capture rhythm, compare translations, study cross-references (e.g., how Psalms foreshadow Christ in Hebrews 1:5-9), and apply its wisdom personally, such as meditating on Psalm 23 daily for comfort amid trials. This honors Scripture's inerrancy, revealing God's character and redemptive plan through artful expression.
What is the significance of the statement in Romans 1:22 that “professing themselves to be wise, they became fools”?

In Romans 1:22, Paul declares, "Although they claimed to be wise, they became fools" (Berean Standard Bible), highlighting the tragic irony of humanity's rebellion against God: those who suppress the truth about the Creator and profess self-derived wisdom descend into folly by exchanging His glory for corruptible images.

This verse forms the heart of Paul's indictment in Romans 1:18-32 on the universal noetic effects of sin—how futile thinking and darkened hearts lead to idolatry and moral decay (Romans 1:21; Romans 1:23). It exposes human autonomy as the root of spiritual foolishness, echoing warnings like "The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge, but fools despise wisdom and discipline" (Proverbs 1:7), and underscores our desperate need for Christ's redeeming light to renew our minds (Ephesians 4:17-24). In everyday terms, it reminds us to humbly submit our intellect to Scripture rather than trusting fallen reason alone.
What does it mean that sin is lawlessness?

Sin is lawlessness, as stated in 1 John 3:4: "Everyone who practices sin practices lawlessness as well. Indeed, sin is lawlessness." This means sin is not merely a mistake or moral failing but a deliberate rebellion against God's holy law, violating His perfect standard of righteousness (James 2:10; Romans 3:20).

Biblically, "lawlessness" (from the Greek *anomia*) describes a state of being without law—active defiance of God's revealed will, whether in thought, word, or deed. It underscores that all sin stems from a heart opposed to God's authority, separating us from Him who is light and in whom is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5).

This truth highlights our desperate need for Christ's penal substitutionary atonement on the cross, where He bore the penalty of our lawlessness to reconcile us to God. Consider confessing specific areas of rebellion in prayer today, claiming God's forgiveness through faith in Jesus (1 John 1:9).
Has anyone ever seen God?

No, no one has ever seen God the Father in His full, unapproachable essence, for He dwells in unapproachable light, and no human can see His face and live (1 Timothy 6:16; Exodus 33:20). The Bible teaches that God has graciously revealed Himself through temporary manifestations, or theophanies, such as to Abraham (Genesis 18:1) and Jacob, who said, "I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been spared" (Genesis 32:30), understood by many as appearances of the pre-incarnate Son. Ultimately, Jesus Christ, who is fully God and fully man, makes the Father known: "No one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is Himself God and is at the Father’s side, has made Him known" (John 1:18), and He declared to Philip, "Whoever has seen Me has seen the Father" (John 14:9). This revelation invites us to behold God's glory in Christ through faith, turning to Him for eternal life (John 17:3).
Are books such as 90 minutes in Heaven and 23 minutes in Hell biblically based?

No, books like *90 Minutes in Heaven* by Don Piper and *23 Minutes in Hell* by Bill Wiese are not biblically based in the authoritative sense; they are personal accounts of claimed near-death experiences that go beyond Scripture and lack the Bible's evidential standards for revelation.

The Bible is God's complete and sufficient Word, once for all delivered to the saints (Jude 1:3), with Jesus as the final revelation through whom God has spoken (Hebrews 1:1-2). Visions or experiences must be tested against its plain teaching (Isaiah 8:20; 1 Thessalonians 5:20-21; Acts 17:11), and these books include sensory details—like specific hellish torments or heavenly scenes—not explicitly described in Scripture, potentially adding to or contradicting it (Deuteronomy 4:2; Revelation 22:18-19). While heaven is real, depicted as God's dwelling with no more pain or death (Revelation 21:3-4), and hell as eternal conscious punishment away from God's presence (Matthew 25:46; Revelation 20:14-15; 2 Thessalonians 1:9), we must anchor our hope there, not in unverified stories.

As a shepherd, I'd encourage you to study passages like Luke 16:19-31 (the rich man and Lazarus) or Revelation 21-22 for God's clear picture of eternity, and prayerfully weigh any experience by the unchanging Word. If you're seeking assurance about heaven or hell, rest in Christ's promise: those who believe in Him have eternal life and will never perish (John 3:16; John 10:28). What draws you to these books—curiosity, comfort, or warning?
Why does God not seem to care about the millions of starving children in the world?

This is a profoundly painful question, one that has grieved sincere hearts for generations, as the sight of innocent children starving pierces our sense of justice and mercy.

You may wonder how a loving, all-powerful God could allow such horrors, or why He doesn't intervene more visibly if He truly cares—a concern echoed in the ancient cry of the psalmist: "How long, O LORD? Will You forget us forever?" (Psalm 13:1). The Bible teaches that God is not indifferent or distant; rather, the root of this suffering lies in humanity's fall into sin through Adam's rebellion, which brought a curse upon creation itself, resulting in thorns, toil, famine, and death (Genesis 3:17-19; Romans 8:20-22). God sovereignly permits evil in this broken age not because He is uncaring, but in His perfect wisdom and justice, weaving even tragedy into a greater purpose of redemption—much like Joseph's brothers' evil intent was overruled for good (Genesis 50:20).

Yet God actively sustains the world, sending rain on the righteous and unrighteous alike (Matthew 5:45), and He calls His people to reflect His compassion through feeding the hungry and caring for orphans (James 1:27; Matthew 25:35-40). Ultimately, He entered our suffering Himself: Jesus, fully God, wept over death (John 11:35), endured hunger in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2), and bore the curse of sin on the cross in our place through penal substitutionary atonement, conquering death by His resurrection so that suffering's sting would one day end (Isaiah 53:4-5; Revelation 21:4).

Christians find hope not in denying the evil, but in trusting God's promise to make all things new at Christ's return, wiping every tear. If this stirs your heart toward Him, turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, offering forgiveness and eternal life where hunger and pain cease forever (John 14:6; Romans 10:9). What aspect of this troubles you most, or draws you closer to seeking Him?

For your own wrestling, read Psalm 34 this week—especially verse 18, "The LORD is near to the brokenhearted"—and pray honestly about the ache, then reach out to a local church for fellowship and practical ways to serve the needy.
What is postmodern therapy, and is it biblical?

Postmodern therapy is a collection of counseling approaches, such as narrative therapy and solution-focused brief therapy, rooted in postmodern philosophy. It views reality, truth, and identity as socially constructed through language and personal stories, often "externalizing" problems (e.g., viewing depression as an invading "black dog" rather than a sin issue) and empowering clients to co-author new, subjective narratives without reference to objective moral absolutes.

This approach is not biblical, as it fundamentally rejects the Bible's claim to objective, inerrant truth as God's inspired Word, which sanctifies us through conformity to His unchanging reality (John 17:17; 2 Timothy 3:16-17). Scripture presents truth as absolute and authoritative from a holy God, not relative or constructed by humans (Psalm 119:160), and it calls us to renew our minds by aligning stories with God's gospel narrative of creation, fall, redemption, and restoration, not rewriting them subjectively (Romans 12:2). While postmodern methods may highlight empathy and client agency—qualities echoing biblical compassion—they undermine core doctrines like God's sovereignty and Scripture's sufficiency for counseling the soul (2 Timothy 3:16-17; Hebrews 4:12).

Faithful Christians pursue soul care through biblical counseling, which integrates God's Word with the relational wisdom of the gospel. If you're seeking help, I encourage finding a certified biblical counselor through the Association of Certified Biblical Counselors (acbc.org) and starting with daily meditation on Psalm 1 for grounding in God's truth. What specific concerns prompted this question?
I am a Christian who is addicted to smoking. How can I quit?

Brother, I hear the weight of your struggle with smoking addiction, and I'm grateful you've reached out—God sees your desire to break free and honors that heart toward holiness. As a believer, you know your body is the temple of the Holy Spirit, bought at a price, so glorify God in it (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). Victory over addiction comes not by willpower alone but through the progressive sanctification of the Holy Spirit, relying on God's faithfulness in providing a way of escape from temptation (1 Corinthians 10:13).

Here are biblically grounded, practical steps to pursue freedom:

1. **Confess and surrender to Christ daily**: Start each morning by praying directly to God, confessing the addiction as bondage and asking for His strength—pour out your heart using Psalm 51 for guidance on repentance and renewal.

2. **Replace the habit with means of grace**: When cravings hit, turn to Scripture meditation (try memorizing Romans 6:11-14 on reckoning yourself dead to sin) or short prayers of dependence on Jesus. Chew gum, drink water, or take a walk while reciting truth aloud.

3. **Seek accountability in your local church**: Share this with a trusted elder, pastor, or mature brother for regular check-ins—James 5:16 teaches that confessing sins to one another brings healing through prayer. Many churches offer addiction recovery groups rooted in the Gospel, like Celebrate Recovery.

4. **Pursue wise physical support**: Consider nicotine replacement therapies or apps for tracking progress, but consult your doctor first for personalized guidance, as I'm not qualified to give medical advice.

Your next actionable step: Tonight, identify one accountability partner from your church, text them to meet this week, and commit to daily tracking your triggers while praying Psalm 119:11 ("I have hidden Your word in my heart that I might not sin against You"). God is faithful to complete the good work He began in you (Philippians 1:6); persevere in grace, and He'll sustain you. How has this addiction affected your walk lately?
What is mortification of sin / the flesh?

Mortification of sin, also called putting to death the flesh, is the believer's Spirit-empowered duty to vigorously combat and subdue the power and deeds of indwelling sin in daily life, as Scripture commands: "For if you live according to the flesh, you will die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live" (Romans 8:13, Berean Standard Bible). This is not self-effort to earn salvation—which comes by justification through faith alone—but a mark of genuine union with Christ in progressive sanctification, where "those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires" (Galatians 5:24).

Biblically, it involves deliberate actions like confessing specific sins promptly (1 John 1:9), fleeing temptations (2 Timothy 2:22), and wielding Scripture as a sword against lusts or anger (Ephesians 6:17; Colossians 3:5). A concrete next step: today, identify one recurring sin pattern in your life, pray for the Spirit's strength while meditating on Romans 6:11 ("count yourselves dead to sin but alive to God in Christ Jesus"), and seek accountability from a trusted brother or sister in your local church to apply it faithfully. God promises grace for this battle, refining us into Christ's likeness with patient mercy (Philippians 1:6).
Is God’s love reckless?

No, God's love is not reckless—it is purposeful, wise, and sovereign, perfectly directed toward the good of His people and His own glory.

The term "reckless love" has gained popularity through a modern worship song, capturing something of God's extravagant pursuit of sinners, like the shepherd leaving the ninety-nine for the one (Luke 15:4-7) or the father running to welcome his prodigal son (Luke 15:20). Yet Scripture portrays God's love (agape) as deliberate and unchanging: "But God proves His love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8, Berean Standard Bible). It cost the Father His only Son (John 3:16), satisfying His justice through penal substitutionary atonement (Romans 3:25-26), not as a careless whim but as the eternal plan of sovereign grace (Ephesians 1:4-5).

This love chases the lost without waste, for God omnisciently knows every heart and outcome. If you're drawn to the song's passion, let it point you to the cross, where love triumphed wisely over sin. What draws you to this question—perhaps a song, a struggle, or something else?
Does the Bible teach that there is a gift of celibacy?

Yes, the Bible teaches that there is a gift of celibacy, presented by the Apostle Paul as one of the diverse spiritual gifts God sovereignly distributes to His people. In 1 Corinthians 7:7, Paul writes, "But I wish everyone were as I am. But each one has his own gift from God; one has this kind of gift, another has that" (Berean Standard Bible), where he explicitly contrasts the "gift" of remaining single for the Lord's service with the "gift" of marriage.

This gift enables undivided devotion to Christ amid life's pressures, as Paul explains: "I want you to be free from concern. The unmarried man is concerned about the things of the Lord, how he can please the Lord. But the married man is concerned about worldly things, how he can please his wife... and his interests are divided" (1 Corinthians 7:32-34). Not everyone receives it—Paul acknowledges most are called to marry (1 Corinthians 7:2, 9)—but for those gifted with celibacy, it frees them for fervent ministry, as seen in Paul's own life and Jesus' example (Matthew 19:12). If you're discerning your own calling, prayerfully study this chapter alongside seeking counsel from your local church elders.
What did it mean that Satan wanted to be like the Most High in Isaiah 14:14?

Isaiah 14:14 describes Satan's prideful ambition to exalt himself to the level of God Himself, seeking to usurp the Most High's unique sovereignty and glory by declaring, "I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will be like the Most High" (Berean Standard Bible). This taunt in Isaiah 14:4-21 is directed at the king of Babylon as a type or symbol, but verses 12-15 vividly portray Satan's prehistoric rebellion and fall from heaven, paralleling Ezekiel 28:12-17 where the anointed cherub sought divine status through self-exaltation.

Theologically, this reveals the root of all sin: creaturely pride aspiring to deity, rejecting God's rightful throne (as echoed in Jesus' words, "I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven" in Luke 10:18, and the war in heaven in Revelation 12:7-9). God opposes such hubris, casting down the rebel (Isaiah 14:15; James 4:6-7), reminding us to humble ourselves before the Lord lest we share in similar folly. For personal application, meditate on Proverbs 16:18 and pray for a heart that fears God alone.
What does the Bible say about the hornet?

The Bible mentions hornets three times, all in the context of God's promise to drive the Canaanite nations out of the Promised Land ahead of Israel. In Exodus 23:28, God declares, "I will send hornets ahead of you which will drive out the Hivites, Perizzites, Canaanites, and Hittites before you." Deuteronomy 7:20 adds, "Moreover, the LORD your God will send hornets among them until even the survivors hiding from you have perished," while Joshua 24:12 reflects on this historically: "I sent the hornet ahead of you which drove the Amorites out before you; it was not by your sword or by your bow."

These references underscore God's sovereign power and faithfulness in conquering enemies on Israel's behalf (Deuteronomy 7:17-24), emphasizing deliverance through divine intervention rather than human strength alone—a reminder that victories in spiritual battles belong to the Lord (Zechariah 4:6).
What were the key events in the life of Jesus Christ?

The life of Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God who became fully human through the Incarnation, is chronicled in the four Gospels as a series of pivotal historical events fulfilling Old Testament prophecies and accomplishing our salvation. Here are the key events in chronological order:

- **Virgin Birth**: Conceived by the Holy Spirit in the Virgin Mary and born in Bethlehem around 4-6 BC, marking God's entry into human history (Matthew 1:18-25; Luke 2:1-7).
- **Visit of the Magi and Flight to Egypt**: Worshipped by wise men from the East, then protected from Herod's massacre of infants by fleeing to Egypt (Matthew 2:1-23).
- **Baptism**: At about age 30, baptized by John the Baptist in the Jordan River, where the Father declared Him the beloved Son and the Holy Spirit descended like a dove (Matthew 3:13-17; Mark 1:9-11; Luke 3:21-22).
- **Temptation**: Led by the Spirit into the wilderness, He overcame Satan's temptations through Scripture, demonstrating His sinless humanity (Matthew 4:1-11; Luke 4:1-13).
- **Public Ministry (c. 3 years)**: Calling disciples, preaching the Kingdom of God (e.g., Sermon on the Mount), performing miracles like healing the sick, feeding multitudes, and raising the dead, and teaching parables (Matthew 4-25; Mark 1-10; Luke 4-19; John 1-12).
- **Transfiguration**: On a mountain, His glory was revealed to Peter, James, and John, with Moses and Elijah appearing alongside Him (Matthew 17:1-8; Mark 9:2-8; Luke 9:28-36).
- **Triumphal Entry**: Rode into Jerusalem on a donkey amid praises of "Hosanna," fulfilling Zechariah 9:9 (Matthew 21:1-11; Mark 11:1-11; Luke 19:28-44; John 12:12-19).
- **Last Supper**: Instituted the Lord's Supper as a memorial of His body and blood, washing the disciples' feet, and predicting betrayal (Matthew 26:17-30; Mark 14:12-26; Luke 22:7-39; John 13-17).
- **Betrayal, Arrest, and Trials**: Betrayed by Judas, arrested in Gethsemane, falsely tried before Jewish leaders and Pilate, scourged, and mocked (Matthew 26:36-27:31; Mark 14:32-15:20; Luke 22:39-23:25; John 18-19:16).
- **Crucifixion and Death**: Nailed to the cross at Calvary around AD 30, bearing the penalty for our sins in penal substitutionary atonement, dying after declaring "It is finished" (Matthew 27:32-56; Mark 15:21-41; Luke 23:26-49; John 19:17-37).
- **Burial**: Laid in Joseph of Arimathea's tomb, sealed and guarded (Matthew 27:57-66; Mark 15:42-47; Luke 23:50-56; John 19:38-42).
- **Resurrection**: Rose bodily from the dead on the third day, conquering sin and death, as confirmed by the empty tomb and eyewitnesses (Matthew 28:1-10; Mark 16:1-8; Luke 24:1-12; John 20:1-18).
- **Post-Resurrection Appearances**: Appeared to disciples over 40 days, commissioning them to make disciples of all nations (Luke 24:13-53; John 20:19-21:25; Acts 1:1-8).
- **Ascension**: Ascended into heaven from the Mount of Olives, promising the Holy Spirit (Mark 16:19; Luke 24:50-53; Acts 1:9-11).

These events form the heart of the Gospel: Christ's obedient life, atoning death, and victorious resurrection secure salvation by grace through faith for all who trust Him (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 10:9). To explore further, read one of the Gospels straight through, such as the Gospel of John.
Do Christians need a day of rest?

Christians do not need to observe a specific mandated day of rest as under the Old Testament Sabbath law, which pointed forward to the ultimate rest found in Christ (Hebrews 4:9-10). This is a tertiary matter where faithful Christians hold differing views based on interpretive emphases in Scripture, allowing for Christian liberty as in Romans 14:5-6: "One person esteems one day as better than another, while another esteems all days alike. Each one must be fully convinced in his own mind."

Two major historic Christian positions include: (1) Lord's Day observance, where Reformed and other traditions set aside Sunday—the day of Christ's resurrection—for worship, rest from ordinary work, and delight in God, honoring the fourth commandment's moral principle in a New Covenant form (Exodus 20:8-11; Isaiah 58:13-14); and (2) fulfillment in Christ, where believers experience ongoing spiritual rest daily through faith, without binding any particular day, as the Sabbath was a shadow of things to come (Colossians 2:16-17).

Whatever your conviction, prioritize rhythmic rest to reflect God's creation pattern (Genesis 2:2-3) and guard against burnout—try committing one day weekly to unplug from work, attend church, and meditate on Scripture like Psalm 23 for renewal in Him.
How can I be a good Christian?

Being a good Christian isn't about striving to earn God's favor through perfect performance, but resting in the grace of Jesus Christ who has already made you right with God through His death and resurrection. As Ephesians 2:8-9 says, "For it is by grace you have been saved through faith, and this not from yourselves; it is the gift of God, not by works, so that no one can boast." Yet, true faith produces good works as fruit, as verse 10 continues: "For we are God's workmanship, created in Christ Jesus to walk in good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do."

The path to growing as a faithful disciple—called progressive sanctification—involves abiding in Christ daily through the ordinary means of grace God provides: His Word, prayer, fellowship with His people, and obedience empowered by the Holy Spirit (John 15:4-5; 2 Timothy 3:16-17). Here are key, practical ways to pursue this:

- **Immerse yourself in Scripture**: Let the Bible renew your mind and guide your steps (Romans 12:1-2). Read it daily, not as a checklist but to hear God's voice.
- **Pray continually**: Pour out your heart to God about sin, gratitude, needs, and praises, relying on the Spirit's help (1 Thessalonians 5:17; Romans 8:26).
- **Join a local church**: Be accountable in a body of believers for worship, the Lord's Supper, baptism if not yet done, and mutual encouragement (Hebrews 10:24-25; Acts 2:42).
- **Repent and obey**: When you sin, confess it quickly, turn from it, and walk in love toward God and others (1 John 1:9; James 1:22).
- **Serve others**: Use your gifts humbly, whether in family, work, or ministry, imitating Christ's servant heart (Galatians 5:13; Matthew 20:28).

Start today with one simple step: Commit to reading one chapter of the Gospel of John each morning for a week, then pray through it afterward, asking God to make those truths real in your life. He who began this good work in you will carry it to completion (Philippians 1:6). Press on with hope—God's grace is sufficient, and He delights in your desire to follow Him. What specific area of growth are you seeking help with first?
What does the Bible say about lawsuits / suing?

The Bible strongly discourages believers from taking fellow Christians to secular courts for lawsuits, urging instead resolution through the church. In 1 Corinthians 6:1-8, Paul rebukes the Corinthian church: "If any of you has a grievance against another, does he dare to go to law before the unrighteous instead of before the saints? Do you not know that the saints will judge the world?... Therefore, if you have disputes about such matters, do you dare to go before the unrighteous to be judged rather than before the saints?" He calls it a shameful failure when brothers sue each other, as it harms the church's witness and forfeits heavenly rewards.

Jesus echoes this in Matthew 5:25-26, teaching to "settle matters quickly with your adversary... lest... you be thrown into prison," emphasizing swift reconciliation over legal battles. Old Testament wisdom supports impartial justice without favoritism (Exodus 23:1-3,6; Proverbs 25:8-10 warns against hasty lawsuits that expose folly).

Practically, pursue mediation through your local church elders first, prayerfully seeking forgiveness and restoration as in Matthew 18:15-17—this honors Christ and models gospel grace to the world. If facing injustice from a non-believer, seek wise counsel while entrusting ultimate justice to God (Romans 12:19).
What is kingdom now teaching?

Kingdom Now teaching, often linked to Dominion Theology or movements like the New Apostolic Reformation, holds that Christians are currently tasked with conquering the world's seven cultural spheres (government, education, media, arts, business, family, and religion) to establish God's kingdom on earth through social and political transformation before Christ's return. Proponents interpret passages like Genesis 1:28 (the dominion mandate) and Matthew 6:10 ("Your kingdom come, Your will be done on earth as it is in heaven") as a present call to victory over earthly institutions, sometimes blending this with postmillennial eschatology where the gospel progressively Christianizes society.

This view is a tertiary doctrine where faithful Christians disagree, as Scripture provides interpretive room on the timing and nature of Christ's kingdom (e.g., Revelation 20:1-6 on the millennium; Romans 14 on liberty in disputable matters). Many historic confessions, including Reformed Baptist ones, emphasize instead that Christ's kingdom advances through the gospel and church while awaiting His personal return to consummate it fully (1 Corinthians 15:24-28), encouraging believers to seek earthly influence through faithful witness rather than coercive dominion. If you're exploring this, prayerfully study these texts and discuss with your local church elders for balanced perspective.
What is The Infinite Way?

The Infinite Way is a mystical spiritual movement founded by Joel S. Goldsmith (1892–1964), a former Christian Scientist who began teaching in the 1930s through lectures, books, and classes. It presents a path to inner realization of God as the infinite, impersonal "I" or divine consciousness within each person, emphasizing contemplative meditation to transcend the illusory material world, sin, sickness, and death.

Goldsmith's core book, *The Infinite Way* (1941), draws selectively from the Bible (especially mystical interpretations of John and Psalms), Mary Baker Eddy, and Eastern thought, teaching that spiritual healing and supply come from attaining "the Christ-mind" or divine identity already present in us.

From the perspective of historic orthodox Christianity, The Infinite Way diverges significantly from core biblical doctrines, such as the personal, triune nature of God (Matthew 28:19), the reality of human sin requiring Christ's penal substitutionary atonement (Romans 3:23-25; Isaiah 53:5-6), and salvation by grace through faith in Christ's historical death and resurrection (Ephesians 2:8-9; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4). The Bible presents Jesus Christ as the exclusive way to the Father, not an inner state to realize (John 14:6; Acts 4:12). If you're exploring this, I encourage studying Scripture directly, perhaps starting with the Gospel of John, and discussing with a pastor grounded in the full counsel of God's Word.
Does God have favorites?

No, God does not have favorites or show partiality; He is perfectly just and impartial in His character and judgments. The Bible declares this clearly: "For God does not show favoritism" (Romans 2:11, Berean Standard Bible), and Peter proclaimed, "God does not show favoritism but accepts people from every nation who fear him and do what is right" (Acts 10:34-35).

God's love extends to all people equally as Creator—He "causes his sun to rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous" (Matthew 5:45)—yet He enters into a saving relationship with those who repent and trust in Christ by faith alone, not based on ethnicity, status, or merit (Ephesians 2:8-9; Galatians 3:28). This means His blessings and discipline flow from His sovereign wisdom and holiness, inviting every heart to draw near through Jesus. If you're wrestling with feelings of being overlooked, rest in His promise: "The Lord does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart" (1 Samuel 16:7). Consider meditating on Psalm 145 today, praising His fairness and turning any burdens over to Him in prayer.
How can I live a holy life?

Living a holy life flows from the grace of God who has already declared you holy through faith in Christ, and it unfolds through progressive sanctification by the Holy Spirit as you walk in obedience (1 Peter 1:15-16; Philippians 2:12-13).

The Bible teaches that holiness is not achieved by your willpower alone but by depending on God's power, using the means of grace He provides: immerse yourself daily in Scripture to renew your mind (Psalm 119:11); pray without ceasing, confessing sin and seeking His strength (1 Thessalonians 5:17); pursue fellowship in a local church for accountability and encouragement (Hebrews 10:24-25); and flee temptation while putting sin to death by the Spirit (Romans 8:13).

A concrete next step: Start each day with 15 minutes in God's Word—read a Psalm and a chapter from the Gospels—then pray specifically, "Lord, make me holy as You are holy today," naming one area of struggle. As you persevere, trust that He who began this good work in you will complete it until Christ's return (Philippians 1:6). You're not alone; draw near to Jesus, our great High Priest who sympathizes with your weaknesses (Hebrews 4:15-16).
What is the sinner's prayer?

The sinner's prayer is an informal, heartfelt prayer commonly used in evangelical Christianity to express repentance from sin and personal faith in Jesus Christ alone for salvation, often modeled after passages like Romans 10:9: "that if you confess with your mouth, 'Jesus is Lord,' and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved."

It is not a magical formula or work that earns salvation—true justification comes by grace alone through faith alone in Christ's atoning death and resurrection (Ephesians 2:8-9)—but serves as a simple way for someone to voice their trust in Him as Savior and Lord, much like the thief on the cross who cried out to Jesus in faith (Luke 23:42). Many have found it a meaningful first step in responding to the Gospel, though the Bible emphasizes ongoing fruit of repentance and obedience as evidence of genuine faith (James 2:17). If you're considering this, read Romans 10 slowly in prayer, then share with a trusted pastor or believer for encouragement and baptism as the next obedient step.
How can a Christian avoid being a doormat for other people?

Christians often wrestle with this tension: Jesus calls us to love others sacrificially (John 13:34-35), yet He Himself set firm boundaries, rebuking Pharisees sharply (Matthew 23:13-33) and withdrawing from manipulative crowds (Mark 1:35-38). Being a doormat enables sin rather than true love, which "does not rejoice in wrongdoing" but rejoices in truth (1 Corinthians 13:6). Here's how to avoid it biblically, blending meekness with wisdom.

First, cultivate discernment through Scripture and prayer. Ask God for wisdom to distinguish between genuine need and exploitation (James 1:5). Not every request deserves a yes; love your neighbor as yourself (Matthew 22:39) includes stewarding your time, energy, and resources as God's gifts.

Second, speak the truth in love plainly (Ephesians 4:15). Practice gracious firmness: "I care about you, but I can't do that right now," or confront directly as in Matthew 18:15. This honors God more than passive resentment.

Third, set proactive boundaries. Prioritize your commitments to God, family, and church (1 Timothy 5:8), saying no early to prevent overload. Build habits like a weekly review: "Does this align with my calling?"

Finally, rest in Christ's example—meek yet majestic (Matthew 11:29). True strength flows from union with Him, not people-pleasing.

**One actionable next step:** This week, identify one draining relationship and prayerfully prepare a loving, truthful response using Ephesians 4:15 as your guide. Share it with a trusted mentor for feedback, then implement. God equips you for this; He who began a good work in you will complete it (Philippians 1:6).
What does it mean that the workers are few (Matthew 9:37)?

In Matthew 9:37, Jesus declares to His disciples, “The harvest is plentiful, but the laborers are few,” using the vivid metaphor of a ripe harvest to describe the abundance of people spiritually ready to receive the Gospel, contrasted with the scarcity of committed workers to gather them in. This statement comes after Jesus compassionately observes the crowds as “distressed and dejected, like sheep without a shepherd” (Matthew 9:36), highlighting the urgent need for more disciples equipped to proclaim the kingdom, teach, heal, and evangelize.

The doctrinal significance underscores God’s sovereign initiative in drawing sinners to salvation through Christ, as echoed in John 4:35 where Jesus urges, “Lift up your eyes and look at the fields, for they are already white for harvest.” Practically, Jesus immediately models the response by instructing prayer: “Therefore pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers into his harvest” (Matthew 9:38). A concrete next step is to join this prayer daily, perhaps listing specific unreached people or fields in your life, while considering how God might call you to labor through sharing the Gospel or supporting missionaries in your local church.
What was the purpose of the Levitical Law?

The Levitical Law, outlined primarily in the book of Leviticus, served to instruct Israel's priests and people in holy worship, ceremonial purity, moral conduct, and sacrificial atonement, setting them apart as God's holy nation amid pagan surroundings. It revealed God's perfect holiness and exposed human sinfulness, acting as a tutor that pointed forward to the ultimate sacrifice of Jesus Christ (Leviticus 11:44 "Be holy because I, the Lord your God, am holy"; Galatians 3:24 "The law was our guardian to lead us to Christ"); Hebrews 10:1 describes it as "a shadow of the good things to come, not the reality itself").

This law underscored the need for substitutionary atonement—foreshadowing Christ's once-for-all offering on the cross—while training Israel in obedience and dependence on God's grace. Today, as believers, we see its fulfillment in Jesus, freeing us to pursue holiness by the Spirit rather than ritual shadows (Hebrews 10:14 "For by a single offering He has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified"). If you're studying this for personal growth, meditate on Leviticus 16 (the Day of Atonement) alongside Hebrews 9-10 for a clear Gospel bridge.
What does the Bible say about confession of sin to a priest?

The Bible instructs believers to confess their sins directly to God, who faithfully forgives through the atoning blood of Jesus Christ, as stated in 1 John 1:9: "If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness." There is no New Testament command or example requiring confession of sins to a human priest; instead, Scripture emphasizes Jesus Christ as the sole mediator between God and humanity (1 Timothy 2:5: "For there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus").

In the Old Testament, priests facilitated certain sin offerings after confession to God (Leviticus 5:5-6), but this ceremonial system was fulfilled and set aside by Christ's once-for-all sacrifice (Hebrews 10:11-14). Under the New Covenant, all believers form a "royal priesthood" with direct access to God through Christ (1 Peter 2:9; Hebrews 4:16), and James 5:16 encourages mutual confession among believers for healing and prayer support, without designating priests as intermediaries.

This underscores justification by faith alone in Christ's finished work, freeing us from any ongoing human mediation for forgiveness. If you're wrestling with guilt, begin by praying directly to God, confessing specifically and trusting His promise in 1 John 1:9.
Should Christians go to nightclubs? Is clubbing a sin?

No, the Bible does not explicitly forbid nightclubs, as they are a modern cultural phenomenon, but Scripture provides clear principles that make clubbing unwise and often sinful for Christians, primarily due to the environments' strong temptations toward drunkenness, sexual immorality, and worldliness.

This falls under Christian liberty (Romans 14:1-23), a tertiary matter where faithful believers may disagree based on personal conscience and context. Some Christians avoid nightclubs entirely, viewing them as places of habitual excess that hinder sanctification and risk becoming a stumbling block to others (Romans 14:13; 1 Thessalonians 5:22). Others might attend rarely and cautiously—such as for a non-drinking social event with believing friends—provided it doesn't lead to sin like intoxication (Ephesians 5:18), lustful gazing (Matthew 5:28), or compromising one's witness (1 Corinthians 10:31-33).

Biblically, the body is God's temple (1 Corinthians 6:19-20), so we must flee sexual immorality and pursue holiness (1 Corinthians 6:18; Hebrews 12:14). Clubbing often normalizes these very dangers through alcohol-fueled dancing, provocative attire, and darkened anonymity. True love for God asks: Does this glorify Him or grieve the Spirit (Ephesians 4:30)?

Examine your heart prayerfully: What draws you there, and does it build up your faith? A wise next step is discussing this with a mature believer or pastor in your local church for accountability, then choosing alternatives like hosting friends for edifying fellowship. God desires your joy in purity, not fleeting thrills (Psalm 16:11).
Does the statue outside the United Nations have any connection to the beast of the end times?

No, the statue known as "The Guardian"—a 23-foot-tall sculpture of a winged, feline-like figure unveiled outside the United Nations headquarters in New York in September 2024 as part of a Brazilian environmental art exhibit—has no direct biblical or prophetic connection to the beast described in Revelation 13.

Some Christians have noted superficial resemblances to the "beast rising out of the sea" in Revelation 13:1-2, which the Bible portrays as a composite creature with features like those of a leopard, bear, and dragon, empowered by Satan to deceive the world and oppose God during the end times. This beast symbolizes a final Antichrist system of global rebellion against Christ, marked by blasphemy and persecution of believers. However, the statue's artist, Sebastian, explicitly describes it as a guardian of life symbolizing protection of the natural world amid climate concerns, with no stated intent to evoke apocalyptic imagery.

Eschatology—the study of end times—is a tertiary doctrine where faithful Christians hold diverse orthodox views, such as premillennialism (Christ returns before a literal thousand-year reign), amillennialism (the millennium is symbolic of the current church age), or postmillennialism (the gospel gradually triumphs before Christ's return). Some see current events like this statue as potential "signs of the times" fulfilling prophecy (Matthew 24:3-14), while others caution against speculative interpretations, emphasizing that Jesus warned against date-setting or overreading symbols apart from clear fulfillment (Matthew 24:36; Acts 1:7). Scripture grants Christian liberty here, urging discernment without division (Romans 14:1-5).

Rather than fixating on symbols, focus on readiness: study Revelation prayerfully in your local church, trust Christ's certain return as conquering King (Revelation 19:11-16), and live faithfully today by sharing the gospel. What aspect of end-times prophecy would you like to explore further?
What is the sign of Jonah?

The sign of Jonah refers to the prophet Jonah's miraculous three days and three nights spent in the belly of a great fish after being thrown overboard during a storm, as recorded in the Book of Jonah. Jesus identified this event as the singular sign He would give to a faithless generation, prefiguring His own death, burial, and bodily resurrection: "For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth" (Matthew 12:40, Berean Standard Bible). This typology underscores the resurrection as the ultimate validation of Christ's deity and victory over death, calling people to repentance just as Nineveh responded to Jonah's preaching (Matthew 12:41; Luke 11:29-32).
What does it mean to walk worthy?

To walk worthy means to live in a manner consistent with the gospel calling and character of God, pursuing holiness and fruitfulness that honors Christ in everyday obedience. The Apostle Paul describes it as walking "in a manner worthy of the calling to which you have been called" (Ephesians 4:1, Berean Standard Bible), and again as walking "in a manner worthy of the Lord, to please Him in every way, bearing fruit in every good work, growing in the knowledge of God" (Colossians 1:10).

This involves progressive sanctification—being transformed by the Holy Spirit through humility, love, patience, and good works that flow from union with Christ (Ephesians 4:2-3; Colossians 1:10-12).
