For they make their phylacteries broad and their fringes long" (Matthew 23:5, Berean Standard Bible). This practice symbolizes constant remembrance of God's law but, in the New Testament, underscores the heart's true devotion over external rituals (Matthew 23:25-28).
Who are true worshipers (John 4:23)?

True worshipers, as Jesus declares to the Samaritan woman, are those who worship the Father **in spirit and truth** (John 4:23-24 Berean Standard Bible). 

This means heartfelt worship from a regenerate spirit empowered by the Holy Spirit—not mere external rituals or location-bound traditions like the Jerusalem temple—and aligned with the truth of God's Word, centered on Christ as the ultimate revelation of the Father (John 1:18; John 14:6). The Father actively seeks such worshipers, inviting all believers to offer spiritual sacrifices through Jesus (1 Peter 2:5; Romans 12:1), whether in private devotion or gathered church life. To grow as one, begin each day praising God through a Scripture-saturated prayer from the heart.
Book of Obadiah - Bible Survey

The Book of Obadiah is the shortest book in the Old Testament, consisting of a single chapter with 21 verses, written by the prophet Obadiah as a divine oracle pronouncing judgment on the nation of Edom for their pride, violence against their brother Jacob (Israel), and failure to aid Jerusalem during its siege. Likely composed shortly after the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BC—though some date it to the ninth century BC following Edom's treachery against Judah during Jehu's revolt—its message centers on God's sovereign justice against nations that exalt themselves and persecute His covenant people.

### Key Themes and Outline
- **Edom's Sin and Pride (Obadiah 1:1-9)**: God exposes Edom's arrogance, declaring, "The pride of your heart has deceived you... Though you soar like the eagle and make your nest among the stars, from there I will bring you down" (Obadiah 1:3-4, Berean Standard Bible). Edom, descended from Esau (Genesis 25:30), had rejoiced in Judah's downfall and looted the helpless, violating the brotherhood covenant (Amos 1:11).
- **Edom's Violence and Judgment (Obadiah 1:10-14)**: Their betrayal on the "day of distress" merits reciprocal destruction, as "as you have done, it will be done to you" (Obadiah 1:15).
- **Deliverance for God's People and Kingdom Hope (Obadiah 1:15-21)**: Israel will possess Edom's territory, and "the kingdom will be the Lord's" (Obadiah 1:21), foreshadowing God's ultimate reign.

Doctrinally, Obadiah underscores God's faithfulness to His elect remnant, His hatred of ethnic pride, and the certainty of divine retribution, echoing broader prophetic themes like the "day of the Lord" (Joel 2:1). For believers today, it offers concrete assurance amid injustice: trust God's perfect justice by meditating on His promises, such as those in Romans 12:19 where vengeance belongs to Him alone.
What does it mean to gain the whole world but lose your soul?

Jesus posed this piercing question in Mark 8:36: "For what does it profit a man to gain the whole world and forfeit his soul?" and similarly in Matthew 16:26. It means achieving ultimate worldly success—wealth, power, fame, or every material desire—yet forfeiting eternal life with God, because the soul's value far surpasses all creation; no earthly gain can ransom it from eternal separation due to sin (Mark 8:37).

In context, Jesus calls disciples to deny self, take up the cross, and follow Him, warning that prioritizing temporal gains over allegiance to Christ leads to soul-loss, while true life is found in Him alone (Mark 8:34-35). The soul, as the eternal essence bearing God's image, faces judgment; only justification by faith in Christ's atoning death and resurrection secures it.

Consider your own pursuits: if chasing career highs or possessions crowds out Christ, pause today—read Mark 8 prayerfully and ask God to realign your heart. Turn from sin, trust Jesus who died and rose for you, and gain true life that outlasts the world. What worldly "gain" might be costing you most right now?
Why did God send the flood when He knew sin would continue after the flood?

You might wonder why God sent the flood in Noah's day if He, in His omniscience, knew humanity's sinfulness would continue afterward, making the judgment seem incomplete or futile. The Bible teaches that the flood was a righteous act of divine judgment against pervasive wickedness, not an attempt to eradicate sin entirely from the earth, but to destroy a corrupt generation, preserve a faithful remnant through Noah, and extend common grace to restrain future evil on a broader scale (Genesis 6:5-7; Genesis 6:11-13).

Scripture describes the pre-flood world as one where "every intent of the thoughts of [man's] heart was only evil continually," grieving God's heart and prompting Him to blot out mankind except for righteous Noah and his family (Genesis 6:5-6, Berean Standard Bible). After the flood, God covenanted never again to destroy the earth with a global flood, acknowledging the ongoing bent of the human heart toward evil while promising seasonal stability as an act of mercy (Genesis 8:21-22; Genesis 9:11). This demonstrates God's holiness demands justice on sin—He is both patient and just—yet His sovereign plan unfolds progressively toward the ultimate defeat of sin at Christ's return (2 Peter 3:5-7; Revelation 21:1-4).

The flood foreshadows the Gospel: just as Noah's ark provided salvation through judgment for those who entered by faith, so Jesus Christ is the ultimate ark, shielding believers from God's wrath through His atoning death and resurrection (1 Peter 3:18-21; Romans 5:9). If this stirs questions about sin's hold or God's judgment in your life, consider reading Genesis 6-9 prayerfully this week, asking the Lord to reveal His grace amid human rebellion—what stands out to you about His mercy there?
Should a church tithe 10% of the offerings it receives?

No, the Bible does not command a local church to tithe 10% of its offerings to other ministries or churches, as this practice stems from Old Testament Israel-specific commands like Leviticus 27:30 and Malachi 3:8-10 rather than New Testament principles for the church. Tithing is a tertiary matter where faithful Christians may disagree in good conscience, guided by the liberty taught in Romans 14:1-6 on disputable matters.

Two major historic Christian views exist: (1) Some, including certain Baptist and evangelical leaders, advocate a church "tithe" of 10% (or more) from general offerings to support missions, church planting, or associations, seeing it as wise stewardship modeling Israel's generosity under the law (1 Corinthians 9:13-14). (2) Others, emphasizing New Testament grace, hold that churches should give proportionally and cheerfully from undesignated funds as needs arise and the Spirit leads, without a mandated percentage (2 Corinthians 8:12; 9:7), prioritizing support for local ministry, the poor, and gospel advance.

In either approach, the aim is faithful stewardship of God's resources for His kingdom. I encourage prayerfully reviewing your church's budget with elders, studying passages like 2 Corinthians 8-9 together, and ensuring giving flows from gospel generosity rather than legalism.
Easter Bible reading plan

Here's a simple 8-day Bible reading plan tracing the events of Passion Week, from Jesus' triumphal entry to His resurrection and early appearances. It draws from all four Gospels for a fuller picture of Christ's atoning death and bodily resurrection, the heart of the Gospel (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Read one passage each day, perhaps aloud, meditating on how Jesus' sacrifice secures forgiveness by grace through faith.

**Day 1: Palm Sunday – Triumphal Entry**  
Matthew 21:1-11; Mark 11:1-11; Luke 19:28-44; John 12:12-19  
*Reflect:* Jesus enters as King, yet rides humbly on a donkey, fulfilling prophecy (Zechariah 9:9).

**Day 2: Cleansing the Temple & Teaching**  
Matthew 21:12-22; Mark 11:12-25; Luke 19:45-48  
*Reflect:* Jesus' zeal for true worship exposes our need for heart cleansing.

**Day 3: Tuesday's Controversies**  
Matthew 21:23-25:46; Mark 11:27-13:37; Luke 20:1-21:4  
*Reflect:* Jesus warns of judgment and Olivet Discourse, urging readiness for His return.

**Day 4: Wednesday – Anointing & Betrayal Plot**  
Matthew 26:1-16; Mark 14:1-11; Luke 22:1-6; John 12:1-8  
*Reflect:* Extravagant devotion contrasts Judas' greed, pointing to Christ's sacrificial love.

**Day 5: Maundy Thursday – Last Supper & Gethsemane**  
Matthew 26:17-46; Mark 14:12-42; Luke 22:7-46; John 13:1-17:26  
*Reflect:* Jesus institutes the Lord's Supper and prays for unity, modeling servant love.

**Day 6: Good Friday – Trial & Crucifixion**  
Matthew 26:47-27:66; Mark 14:43-15:47; Luke 22:47-23:56; John 18:1-19:42  
*Reflect:* Penal substitutionary atonement—Christ bears our sin's penalty on the cross (Isaiah 53:5-6).

**Day 7: Holy Saturday – In the Tomb**  
Matthew 27:57-66; Luke 23:50-56; John 19:38-42  
*Reflect:* Silence before victory, reminding us God works even in waiting (Psalm 22:1).

**Day 8: Easter Sunday – Resurrection & Appearances**  
Matthew 28:1-20; Mark 16:1-20; Luke 24:1-53; John 20:1-21:25  
*Celebrate:* Christ's bodily resurrection defeats death, securing justification for all who believe (Romans 4:25).

As you read, pray for the Holy Spirit to make these historical events alive in your heart, revealing Christ's victory over sin. For a concrete next step, share one insight from Day 1 with a fellow believer or your local church this week, and attend an Easter service to worship together. He is risen indeed!
What is ignosticism? What is an ignostic?

Ignosticism, also known as igtheism, is a philosophical stance asserting that discussions about the existence or non-existence of God are meaningless or premature until a precise, operational definition of "God" is provided, since varying conceptions (such as a personal deity versus an abstract force) render the question incoherent.

An ignostic is someone who holds this view, typically suspending judgment on theism, atheism, or agnosticism and insisting on definitional clarity first, often critiquing religious language as vague or equivocal.

From a biblical perspective, Scripture offers a clear, self-revealed definition of God as the eternal, sovereign Creator who declares, "I AM WHO I AM" (Exodus 3:14), and who has definitively revealed Himself in the person of Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh (John 1:1, 14). This makes the question of His existence not merely meaningful but urgently answerable through faith in His historical words and works, as the Apostle John urges: "These things are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in His name" (John 20:31). If you're exploring this amid doubts about God, consider reading the Gospel of John prayerfully for that clarity.
What does it mean that Jesus is the Lord of the Sabbath?

Jesus' declaration that He is "Lord of the Sabbath" means He possesses divine authority over the Sabbath, the day of rest God instituted at creation, giving Him the right to define its true purpose, override human traditions, and fulfill its intent in Himself.

This statement comes amid conflicts with the Pharisees, who accused Jesus of breaking the Sabbath by allowing His disciples to pluck grain (Mark 2:23-26) and by healing a man with a withered hand (Mark 3:1-6). Jesus responds by citing David's precedent in eating the consecrated bread (1 Samuel 21:1-6) and then asserts in Mark 2:27-28, "The Sabbath was made for man, and not man for the Sabbath. Therefore the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath." Parallel accounts appear in Matthew 12:8 and Luke 6:5.

Theologically, this underscores Christ's deity and lordship, as only the Creator God who established the Sabbath (Genesis 2:2-3; Exodus 20:8-11) could claim such supremacy. It reveals the Sabbath's merciful design for human rest and refreshment, pointing forward to the ultimate rest believers find in Christ through faith (Hebrews 4:9-10). For us today, it frees believers from legalistic observance under the new covenant, calling us instead to worship Christ as our Sabbath rest—practically, by trusting Him daily amid life's labors rather than rigid rules.
What does it mean that Jesus Christ conquered death?

Jesus Christ conquered death through His bodily resurrection on the third day after His crucifixion, proving His divine power over sin, the grave, and Satan, as foretold in Scripture and witnessed by hundreds (1 Corinthians 15:3-8; Acts 2:24).

This victory, central to the Gospel, validates His atoning sacrifice on the cross—where He bore the penalty for our sins—and guarantees justification by faith for all who trust Him, securing their own future resurrection to eternal life (Romans 4:25; 1 Corinthians 15:54-57). Death no longer holds ultimate power; as the Bible declares, "Death has been swallowed up in victory... But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ!" (1 Corinthians 15:54, 57, Berean Standard Bible).

For every believer, this means confident hope amid grief or fear: your loved ones in Christ will rise, and you will too, transformed forever (1 Thessalonians 4:13-18). If you have not yet trusted in Christ's death and resurrection for forgiveness, turn to Him today—repent of sin and believe in Jesus alone for new life that death cannot touch (John 11:25-26; Romans 10:9). What aspect of this victory stirs your heart most?
What are the books of the Bible? What does it mean that the Bible is composed of different books?

The Bible consists of 66 distinct books—39 in the Old Testament and 27 in the New Testament—divided traditionally as follows:

**Old Testament**  
*Pentateuch (Law):* Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy  
*Historical Books:* Joshua, Judges, Ruth, 1 Samuel, 2 Samuel, 1 Kings, 2 Kings, 1 Chronicles, 2 Chronicles, Ezra, Nehemiah, Esther  
*Wisdom and Poetry:* Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, Song of Solomon  
*Major Prophets:* Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, Daniel  
*Minor Prophets:* Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi  

**New Testament**  
*Gospels:* Matthew, Mark, Luke, John  
*History:* Acts  
*Pauline Epistles:* Romans, 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 Thessalonians, 2 Thessalonians, 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, Philemon  
*General Epistles:* Hebrews, James, 1 Peter, 2 Peter, 1 John, 2 John, 3 John, Jude  
*Prophecy:* Revelation  

That the Bible is "composed of different books" means it is a divinely inspired library or anthology of writings penned by around 40 human authors (such as Moses, David, Paul, and others) over roughly 1,500 years (from about 1400 BC to AD 100), in diverse literary genres including history, law, poetry, prophecy, biography, and letters. Despite their variety in style, audience, and historical context, these books form one unified, cohesive revelation of God's redemptive plan culminating in Jesus Christ, as they are "God-breathed" and profitable for teaching, reproof, correction, and training in righteousness (2 Timothy 3:16-17, Berean Standard Bible). This canonical collection was progressively recognized by the early church through the guidance of the Holy Spirit. To begin exploring it, consider reading one book at a time, such as starting with the Gospel of John for a clear portrait of Christ.
What is the creation mandate / cultural mandate?

The Creation Mandate, also known as the Cultural Mandate, is God's command to humanity immediately after creating Adam and Eve, charging them to exercise stewardship over the earth as His image-bearers. In Genesis 1:28, God blessed them and said to them, "Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth and subdue it; rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air and every creature that crawls upon the earth" (Berean Standard Bible).

This mandate establishes humanity's vocation to develop and cultivate creation through procreation, work, innovation, and dominion, reflecting God's creative order before the fall (Genesis 1:26-30; 2:15). Though sin disrupted this calling (Genesis 3:17-19), it endures as a divine responsibility for believers today—redeemed in Christ to glorify God in family, labor, arts, science, and society (Genesis 1:27; Colossians 3:23). A practical step: reflect on how your daily work stewards God's good creation, perhaps by reading Genesis 1-2 this week and journaling one way to "subdue" your sphere faithfully.
What does it mean that friendship with the world is enmity with God (James 4:4)?

James 4:4 declares, "You adulteresses! Do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God? Therefore, whoever chooses to be a friend of the world renders himself an enemy of God?" (Berean Standard Bible). Here, "friendship with the world" means deliberately embracing the sinful values, priorities, and pursuits of the ungodly system opposed to God—often called the "kosmos" in Scripture—not the physical creation itself, but its rebellious mindset of pride, lust, and self-exaltation (1 John 2:15-17).

This verse warns believers against spiritual adultery, like ancient Israel's unfaithfulness by chasing foreign gods, urging undivided loyalty to God alone. It highlights the incompatibility between loving Christ and conforming to worldly patterns that defy His lordship (Romans 12:2). Practically, examine your affections today: what entertainment, ambitions, or relationships pull you toward selfishness? Replace them by immersing in God's Word and prayer, drawing near to Him as James urges in the next verses (James 4:8).
Why did God harden Pharaoh’s heart?

God hardened Pharaoh's heart as a sovereign act of judgment, confirming Pharaoh's prior stubborn rebellion against Him (Exodus 7:13; Exodus 8:15 where Pharaoh first hardens his own heart), to multiply miraculous signs against Egypt and proclaim God's name throughout the earth (Exodus 7:3; Exodus 9:16). 

This judicial hardening—rooted in Pharaoh's repeated refusal to repent—demonstrated God's supreme power over Pharaoh and Egypt's false gods, securing Israel's deliverance through the Exodus and foreshadowing His righteous judgment on all who oppose Him (Romans 9:17-18). As the Apostle Paul explains, it reveals God's mercy and hardening as expressions of His unchallenged authority, inviting us today to humbly trust His wise purposes rather than resist like Pharaoh (Romans 9:14-21). If you're wrestling with this, meditate on Exodus 9:16 alongside Romans 9 and pray for insight into God's justice and grace.
How should we treat our enemies according to Proverbs 25:21–22?

Proverbs 25:21-22 instructs us to treat our enemies with unexpected kindness: "If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him drink. For in so doing, you will heap burning coals on his head, and the LORD will reward you." This wisdom calls believers to overcome evil with good, mirroring Jesus' command to love our enemies and pray for those who persecute us (Matthew 5:44), as Paul later quotes in Romans 12:20 to urge non-retaliation.

The phrase "heap burning coals" likely evokes an ancient Egyptian gesture of repentance or profound shame that softens the heart toward reconciliation, while promising God's reward for such grace-filled obedience. In practice, when facing hostility—perhaps a harsh colleague or estranged family member—start by praying for them daily (Matthew 5:44), then seek a tangible way to serve their need, like offering help without expectation of thanks, trusting God to work in their conscience and bless your faithfulness.
What does the Bible say about indecision / being indecisive?

The Bible portrays indecision, especially in matters of faith and obedience, as a form of double-mindedness that leads to spiritual instability and ineffectiveness. James warns, "But let him ask in faith, with no doubting, for the one who doubts is like a wave of the sea that is driven and tossed by the wind. For that person must not suppose that he will receive anything from the Lord; he is a double-minded man, unstable in all his ways" (James 1:6-8, Berean Standard Bible). Similarly, Proverbs urges decisive trust in God over self-reliance: "Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and lean not on your own understanding; in all your ways acknowledge Him, and He will make your paths straight" (Proverbs 3:5-6, Berean Standard Bible).

Elijah confronted Israel's indecision sharply: "How long will you waver between two opinions? If the Lord is God, follow Him. But if Baal, then follow him" (1 Kings 18:21, Berean Standard Bible), calling for wholehearted commitment. Joshua echoed this: "Choose for yourselves this day whom you will serve... But as for me and my house, we will serve the Lord" (Joshua 24:15, Berean Standard Bible).

In practice, God invites us to seek His wisdom decisively through prayer and His Word, promising clarity to the single-minded. If you're facing indecision today, pause to pray James 1:5 specifically, asking God for wisdom while confessing any divided loyalties, and consider sharing the decision with a trusted believer for godly counsel (Proverbs 11:14).
What does it mean that God will wipe away every tear (Revelation 21:4)?

Revelation 21:4 declares, "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away" (Berean Standard Bible). This promise describes God's intimate, personal consolation in the new heavens and new earth, the eternal dwelling where He makes all things new, fully reversing the curse of sin that brought death, sorrow, and suffering into creation (Revelation 21:1-5).

Here, the sovereign God acts as tender shepherd, eliminating every source of grief—not merely drying tears but eradicating their causes through Christ's final victory (Isaiah 25:8; Revelation 7:17). For believers enduring trials now, this assures progressive sanctification leads to glorification, where joy is perfect and unending (Romans 8:18-23). Meditate on this verse daily amid hardship, letting it anchor your hope in God's faithfulness.
What is the meaning of “there but for the grace of God go I”?

The phrase “there but for the grace of God go I” expresses profound humility, meaning that apart from God's sustaining grace, I could easily find myself in the same sinful, broken, or tragic situation as the person I'm observing. It originated with 16th-century English reformer John Bradford, who uttered a version of it while watching criminals led to execution, recognizing his own potential for such a path without Christ's mercy.

Biblically, it echoes our universal sinfulness—“for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God” (Romans 3:23, BSB)—and salvation by grace alone: “For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves; it is the gift of God” (Ephesians 2:8, BSB). In practice, it fosters compassion: the next time you see someone's struggle, pause to thank God for His grace in your life and pray for theirs, reminding yourself that perseverance in holiness is His work, not yours (Philippians 1:6, BSB).
How can we walk in God’s ways (Psalm 128:1)?

Walking in God's ways, as blessed in Psalm 128:1—"Blessed are all who fear the LORD, who walk in His ways!"—means living in reverent obedience to His commands from a heart transformed by Christ, pursuing progressive sanctification through the Holy Spirit's power.

Here is how to do so practically, rooted in Scripture:

1. **Fear the Lord through intimate knowledge of His Word.** Begin each day meditating on Scripture, which reveals His ways (Psalm 119:105—"Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path"). Actionable step: Commit to reading one chapter daily from Psalms or Proverbs, journaling one command to apply that day, such as loving your neighbor (Matthew 22:39).

2. **Obey His commands in daily decisions.** God's ways include loving Him fully and others sacrificially (Deuteronomy 10:12-13; Micah 6:8—"He has shown you, O man, what is good. And what does the LORD require of you but to act justly, to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your God?"). Examine your speech, work, relationships, and stewardship—repent where you stray, and take one concrete step today, like forgiving someone who wronged you (Ephesians 4:32).

3. **Rely on the means of grace.** Walk in His ways by prayer, fellowship, and the ordinances. Pray without ceasing for strength (1 Thessalonians 5:17; Philippians 4:6), join a local church for accountability and encouragement (Hebrews 10:24-25), and partake in the Lord's Supper to remember Christ's enabling grace (1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

4. **Trust Christ's righteousness and the Spirit's empowerment.** You cannot walk perfectly in your strength—rest in justification by faith alone (Romans 5:1), knowing true believers persevere by God's keeping power (Philippians 1:6; Jude 1:24).

Start small today: Pick one verse like Psalm 1:1-3, pray it back to God, and share with a trusted believer for mutual encouragement. God promises blessing and fruitfulness to those who walk this path, for His yoke is easy and His burden light (Matthew 11:30). Press on in His grace!
What does the Bible say about the ice age?

The Bible does not mention an "Ice Age" by name, as that is a modern geological term, but it describes ice, frost, snow, and cold weather as part of God's sovereign creation and control over nature. For example, Job records God asking, "From whose womb does the ice emerge, and who gives birth to the frost from the heavens when He made the constellations? He freezes the face of the deep; He spreads the frost upon the waters" (Job 38:29-30, Berean Standard Bible), emphasizing His power over climatic extremes.

Some Bible-believing scholars interpret the global Flood of Noah's day (Genesis 6-9) as the trigger for post-Flood ice age conditions, noting warmer oceans from subterranean waters bursting forth (Genesis 7:11) combined with volcanic ash and cooler summers could have caused rapid glaciation lasting centuries within the biblical timeline of roughly 4,500 years ago. This aligns with the plain historical reading of Genesis 1-11, where God created a mature earth in six literal days (Genesis 1:31; Exodus 20:11) and catastrophically reshaped it through the Flood, upholding Scripture's authority over secular timelines that span millions of years. For deeper study, explore Genesis 8:22, where God promises seasonal stability after the Flood: "As long as the earth endures, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night shall not cease."
What is the significance of the city of Nineveh in the Bible?

Nineveh, the ancient capital of the Assyrian Empire (Genesis 10:11-12), holds profound significance in the Bible as a showcase of God's mercy toward repentant pagans and His sovereign justice against unrepentant evil. In the Book of Jonah, God sent the reluctant prophet Jonah to warn its wicked inhabitants of impending judgment, and the entire city—from King Ashurbanipal down to the animals—repented in sackcloth and ashes, prompting God to relent from destroying them (Jonah 3:5-10). This event strikingly illustrates divine compassion extending to Gentiles, challenging Israel's exclusivity and foreshadowing the Gospel's global reach (Jonah 4:11).

Later, the prophet Nahum foretold Nineveh's total destruction as judgment for its cruelty, violence, and idolatry, which was fulfilled historically around 612 BC when Babylonians and Medes razed the city (Nahum 1:1; 3:7, 19). Theologically, Nineveh exemplifies God's patience with sinners (2 Peter 3:9), the power of prophetic preaching to produce repentance, and the certainty of divine retribution, reminding believers today that no empire or individual escapes God's righteous rule. For deeper study, read Jonah and Nahum alongside Genesis 10 to see its roots in post-Flood rebellion.
What is Epicureanism? What did Epicurus teach?

Epicureanism is a philosophical school founded by the Greek thinker Epicurus (341–270 BC) in Athens, which teaches that the highest good is pleasure, understood primarily as the absence of physical pain (aponia) and mental disturbance (ataraxia), achieved through simple living, moderation, and rational thought.

Epicurus taught that the universe consists of atoms moving randomly in a void (a materialistic view rejecting divine design), that the gods exist but are distant and uninvolved in human affairs, and that death should not be feared since it is merely the cessation of sensation ("death is nothing to us"). He emphasized friendship, self-sufficiency, and avoiding public life or superstitious fears of divine punishment to attain tranquility, famously summarized in his "Tetrapharmakon" (four-part cure): don't fear the gods, don't fear death, the good is easy to obtain, the painful is easy to endure. From a biblical perspective, this contrasts sharply with Scripture's portrayal of a sovereign Creator God who is intimately involved in human affairs and calls us to find ultimate joy in knowing Him, as in Psalm 16:11: "You make known to me the path of life; in Your presence there is fullness of joy; at Your right hand are pleasures forevermore."
Book of Philippians - Bible Survey

The Book of Philippians is a joyful epistle written by the Apostle Paul, along with Timothy, to the church in Philippi around AD 60-62 while Paul was imprisoned in Rome. This congregation, the first church Paul planted in Europe during his second missionary journey (Acts 16:12-40), sent him financial support and a gift delivered by Epaphroditus, prompting Paul to express profound gratitude, encourage perseverance amid suffering, urge unity and humility modeled by Christ, and warn against legalistic false teachers.

### Key Themes and Doctrinal Significance
- **Joy in Christ Amid Trials**: Paul repeatedly emphasizes joy (mentioned 16 times), rooted not in circumstances but in gospel partnership and Christ's lordship, as in "Rejoice in the Lord always. I will say it again: Rejoice!" (Philippians 4:4). This underscores that true joy flows from justification by faith and the advance of the gospel, even through imprisonment or persecution (Philippians 1:12-18).
- **Christ's Humility and Exaltation**: The famous "Christ hymn" (Philippians 2:5-11) portrays Jesus' incarnation, obedient death on the cross, and exaltation, calling believers to "have the same mindset as Christ Jesus" in humility and service—key to penal substitutionary atonement and sanctification.
- **Gospel-Centered Living**: Paul recounts his pursuit of Christ over religious credentials (Philippians 3:4-11), pressing toward the upward call (Philippians 3:12-14), and affirms heavenly citizenship (Philippians 3:20-21), highlighting perseverance of the saints and hope in Christ's return.
- **Unity and Contentment**: Exhortations to unity (Philippians 2:1-4; 4:2-3) and contentment in all situations (Philippians 4:11-13) reflect the fruit of the Spirit in fellowship.

### Brief Outline
1. **Greeting and Thanksgiving** (Philippians 1:1-11): Paul's prayer for their love and discernment.
2. **Gospel Advance in Suffering** (Philippians 1:12-30): Paul's chains serve Christ; live worthy of the gospel.
3. **Humility and Unity** (Philippians 2:1-30): Christ's example; commend Timothy and Epaphroditus.
4. **Knowing Christ Over Religion** (Philippians 3:1-21): Reject legalism; citizenship in heaven.
5. **Final Exhortations and Thanks** (Philippians 4:1-23): Stand firm, rejoice, receive God's peace and provision.

Philippians models rejoicing in trials as a witness to the gospel's power, offering believers today a concrete step: meditate daily on Philippians 4:4-9, praying through these verses to cultivate joy and peace amid personal challenges.
In what ways was Moses like Jesus?

Moses was a type or foreshadowing of Jesus Christ, serving as a prophet, deliverer, mediator, and leader whom God raised up for His people, as Scripture explicitly states in Deuteronomy 18:15: "The Lord your God will raise up for you a Prophet like me from among you, from your brothers; you shall listen to Him." Key similarities include their miraculous preservations at birth amid genocidal threats (Exodus 1:15-2:10; Matthew 2:13-18), deliverance of Israel from bondage (Exodus 12-14; through Christ's atonement freeing sinners from sin's slavery, Romans 6:6), performance of authenticating miracles (plagues and Red Sea crossing in Exodus 7-14; Jesus' signs in John 20:30-31), forty-day fasts in preparation for ministry (Exodus 34:28; Matthew 4:1-2), and faithful intercession for rebellious Israel (Exodus 32:11-14, 32; Hebrews 7:25).

Both mediated God's covenants—Moses at Sinai with the law (Exodus 19-24), and Jesus inaugurating the new covenant through His blood (Hebrews 8:6-13; Luke 22:20)—and faced initial rejection by their own people before vindication (Exodus 2:11-15; John 1:11). Hebrews 3:1-6 draws the ultimate contrast and fulfillment: "Jesus has been counted worthy of more glory than Moses... For every house is built by someone, but the builder of the house is Christ. Moses was faithful as a servant in all God’s house... but Christ is faithful as the Son over God’s house." This typology points to Christ's supremacy, inviting us to consider Him as the greater Prophet, Priest, and King we must heed for salvation (Acts 3:22-23).
What is Socinianism?

Socinianism is a 16th- and 17th-century anti-Trinitarian theological movement originating in Poland, named after Italian thinkers Laelius Socinus (1525–1562) and his nephew Faustus Socinus (1539–1604), who systematized its rationalistic rejection of core Christian doctrines.

Key tenets include denying the Trinity (insisting God is one person, not three co-equal persons), rejecting Christ's eternal deity and pre-existence (viewing Jesus as a mere human prophet empowered by God), opposing penal substitutionary atonement (replacing it with a moral influence theory), and dismissing original sin while emphasizing free will over divine sovereignty in salvation. This places Socinianism outside orthodox Christianity, as it contradicts the Bible's clear teaching on God's triune nature (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14), Christ's full divinity and humanity (John 1:1,14; Colossians 2:9; Philippians 2:6-8), and salvation by Christ's sacrificial death (Isaiah 53:5-6; Romans 3:25; Hebrews 2:17). Historic confessions like the 1689 London Baptist Confession affirm these truths against such errors, urging believers to cling to Scripture's witness for sound doctrine (2 Timothy 3:16-17).
Can an unmarried man be a deacon or elder?

Yes, an unmarried man can qualify as a deacon or elder according to the biblical qualifications, as Scripture does not explicitly require marriage but emphasizes proven godly character and faithfulness. The key passages are 1 Timothy 3:1-13 for overseers (elders) and deacons, and Titus 1:5-9 for elders, where the phrase "husband of one wife" (1 Timothy 3:2, 12; Titus 1:6) literally means "one-woman man" in Greek, pointing to sexual purity and fidelity rather than mandating marriage itself—this rules out polygamy, promiscuity, or divorce/remarriage issues but applies to singles who demonstrate such integrity in their singleness.

The requirement to "manage his own household well, keeping his children under control with all dignity" (1 Timothy 3:4-5, 12; Titus 1:6) shows proven leadership in one's sphere of responsibility; for unmarried men without children, this translates to self-management, relationships, and any dependents under their care, as Paul himself was unmarried yet appointed elders (1 Corinthians 7:7-8, 32-35; 9:5).

Faithful Christians hold varying views here: some traditions (like certain Presbyterians) prefer or require married men with children as the fullest demonstration of these qualities, while others (including many Baptists and Reformed congregations) affirm qualified singles, following Paul's example and the principle that God calls diverse servants. Discuss this with your local church elders, who apply these Scriptures in context, and prayerfully pursue character growth through the means of grace like Scripture, prayer, and fellowship regardless of marital status.
Is it better, spiritually, to be poor than to be rich?

No, Scripture does not teach that poverty is spiritually superior to wealth; what matters is the posture of the heart toward God, whether one is rich or poor.

Jesus warns of wealth's spiritual dangers, as it can choke the word and make entering God's kingdom difficult—like a camel passing through the eye of a needle (Matthew 19:23-24)—yet He also blesses faithful poor believers, choosing them as heirs of the kingdom (James 2:5). Conversely, God richly blessed faithful servants like Abraham and Job, entrusting them with wealth as stewardship to honor Him. The love of money is the root of all kinds of evil (1 Timothy 6:10), so both poverty and riches test us: poverty toward discontent and envy, riches toward pride and idolatry. Godliness with contentment is great gain in any circumstance (1 Timothy 6:6; Philippians 4:11-13).

Faithful Christians across traditions agree on pursuing Christ above possessions, though some (like certain monastic orders) emphasize voluntary poverty as a helpful discipline, while others see generous stewardship of wealth as equally obedient. Examine your heart this week: pray through Proverbs 30:8-9, asking God for neither poverty nor riches but daily bread, and consider one generous act to steward what He has given you.
Will the sun really be turned to darkness and the moon to blood (Joel 2:31)?

The Bible teaches in Joel 2:31 that the sun will be turned to darkness and the moon to blood as cosmic signs preceding the great and awesome Day of the Lord, a prophetic event echoed by Peter in Acts 2:20 and paralleled in Matthew 24:29 and Revelation 6:12.

You may wonder if this is literal cosmic upheaval or symbolic apocalyptic language for divine judgment, a sincere question given modern astronomy's view of stable heavenly bodies. Faithful Christians hold varied orthodox interpretations on these tertiary eschatological details: some see partial fulfillment in Pentecost's outpouring (Acts 2:16-21, where Peter applies Joel to the church age's judgments), others anticipate a future literal darkening as Christ returns visibly (Revelation 1:7). Scripture presents this as certain under God's sovereign control, urging readiness through repentance and faith in Christ, who secures us for that Day (1 Thessalonians 5:1-11). What aspect of this prophecy stirs you most?
How can I overcome spiritual apathy?

Spiritual apathy is a heavy burden many believers face, a dimming of our first love for Christ amid life's distractions and the pull of our sinful flesh, but the good news is that God stirs the embers through His Spirit and the means of grace (Revelation 2:4; Galatians 5:17).

The Bible points to renewal not by sheer willpower, but by returning to Christ who secured our justification by faith alone and empowers progressive sanctification. Start by honestly confessing your apathy to God in prayer, as He promises to revive the humble heart (Psalm 51:10-12; Isaiah 57:15). Immerse yourself daily in Scripture, perhaps beginning with the Psalms or Gospel of John, meditating on truths like Christ's unchanging love in Romans 8:38-39. Prioritize unhurried prayer, not as a duty but a delight in fellowship with the Father through the Son. Join a local church for mutual encouragement, accountability, and the Lord's Supper, where Christ's spiritual presence strengthens us (Hebrews 10:24-25; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26).

A concrete next step: Today, set aside 15 minutes to read Psalm 42 aloud, then pray it personally, asking God to restore your thirst for Him, and text a trusted brother or sister in Christ to pray with you this week. He who began this good work in you will complete it, so persevere in hope (Philippians 1:6).
How can I experience healing and recovery after an abortion?

I'm so sorry for the deep pain, grief, and guilt you're carrying after your abortion; the Lord draws near to the brokenhearted and saves those crushed in spirit (Psalm 34:18). Abortion takes the life of an unborn child, whom God fearfully and wonderfully made in the womb (Psalm 139:13-16), and it's a grievous sin against His holy design, yet His mercy is greater still—He offers complete forgiveness and healing through Jesus Christ's atoning death on the cross for sinners like us.

The Bible assures us that if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness (1 John 1:9). As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us (Psalm 103:12). True healing begins with repentance—turning from sin to trust fully in Christ's finished work, not our own efforts—and justification by faith alone brings peace with God (Romans 5:1).

Here's a path forward rooted in Scripture:
- **Confess directly to God in prayer today**: Pour out your heart honestly, naming the sin specifically, claiming Christ's blood-bought forgiveness, and asking for His comfort. Meditate on Psalm 51, David's prayer after his grave sin, as a model.
- **Immerse in God's Word**: Read daily passages like Isaiah 43:25 ("I, even I, am He who blots out your transgressions for My own sake; and I will not remember your sins") and 2 Corinthians 1:3-4, where God comforts us in affliction so we can comfort others.
- **Seek godly community**: Confess your sins to trusted believers for healing prayer, as the prayer of a righteous person is powerful and effective (James 5:16). Reach out to your local church elders or a certified biblical counselor through the Association of Certified Biblical Counselors (ACBC) for personalized support—many churches have post-abortion recovery ministries.
- **Rest in progressive sanctification**: The Holy Spirit will grow you through the means of grace (Scripture, prayer, fellowship), replacing shame with joy over time.

Your next step: Tonight, kneel before the Lord in prayer using Psalm 51, then tomorrow contact a faithful pastor or ACBC counselor (find one at biblicalcounseling.com). God is the God of restoration; He can make all things new in Christ (Revelation 21:5), and countless women have found freedom this way. You're not alone—He holds you tenderly even now.
What does God mean when He says, “Seek me and live” (Amos 5:4)?

In Amos 5:4, God urgently calls Israel to turn from idolatry, empty rituals, and injustice by wholeheartedly seeking Him in repentance and obedience, promising true life amid impending judgment. The Berean Standard Bible renders it: "For this is what the LORD says to the house of Israel: 'Seek Me and live!'" This contrasts their superficial religion (Amos 5:21-24)—sacrifices without mercy and justice—with genuine pursuit of God, who alone offers deliverance from death (Amos 5:6-8).

Biblically, "seek me" echoes Deuteronomy 4:29, where wholehearted seeking leads to finding God, and anticipates the New Testament call to repent and believe for salvation (Acts 17:30; Luke 11:9-10). It points to life not just physical survival, but spiritual vitality and eternal security through faith in Christ, the true source of life (John 10:10; John 14:6).

Today, this invites you to seek God through prayerful confession of sin, immersion in His Word, and trust in Jesus' atoning death and resurrection. A practical next step: Set aside 10 minutes daily to read Amos 5 and pray, "Lord, help me seek You above all else for true life."
If aborted babies go to heaven, why is abortion wrong?

You may wonder, if God receives aborted babies into heaven—as many Christians believe He does, based on passages like David’s confidence that his deceased infant son would be with him (2 Samuel 12:23) and the grace of Christ’s atonement covering those incapable of personal faith—then what makes abortion truly wrong?

Abortion is gravely sinful because it deliberately ends the life of an innocent human being made in God’s image (Genesis 1:27; Genesis 9:6), violating the sixth commandment: “You shall not murder” (Exodus 20:13). The eternal destination of the victim does not justify the act; God condemns murder regardless, as seen when Cain slew righteous Abel, whose blood cried out from the ground for justice (Genesis 4:10). Taking life usurps God’s sovereign authority as the giver and taker of life (Job 1:21; Deuteronomy 32:39), treating His image-bearers as disposable rather than precious.

True compassion mourns both the child’s stolen earthly life—robbing them of breath, growth, relationships, and purpose in God’s world—and the spiritual harm to those involved, yet offers hope: no sin is beyond Christ’s forgiving blood. If this weighs on your heart, repent and trust Jesus, who died for murderers and all sinners, granting new life (1 John 1:9). Consider joining a local church ministry that supports women in crisis pregnancies or advocates for the unborn, walking out the gospel’s value for every life.
Should communion be open or closed?

The question of open versus closed communion (also called the Lord's Supper) is a longstanding secondary matter among orthodox Christians, where faithful believers may hold differing practices without compromising core gospel truths. Open communion welcomes any professing Christian (often regardless of baptismal status or church membership) to participate, emphasizing Christ's universal invitation in passages like Matthew 11:28; closed (or "close") communion restricts participation to baptized believers in good standing with the local church, typically requiring membership or endorsement, to honor 1 Corinthians 11:27-29's call to self-examination and discerning "the body of Christ" (the church).

Historically, paedobaptist traditions like Presbyterianism and Lutheranism tend toward open communion, viewing baptism as the primary prerequisite, while Baptist and Reformed Baptist churches favor closed or close(d) communion to uphold believer's baptism by immersion as an obedient picture of inward grace (Acts 2:41; Romans 6:3-4). As a Reformed Baptist perspective, informed by the 1689 London Baptist Confession (Chapter 30), I see close(d) communion as a faithful safeguard: it protects the ordinance's meaning as a sign of covenant unity among regenerate believers, preventing unworthy participation while fostering church discipline and mutual accountability.

Scripture does not mandate one practice dogmatically, prioritizing instead reverence, self-judgment, and proclamation of Christ's death (1 Corinthians 11:23-26). In keeping with Christian liberty on disputable matters (Romans 14:1-6), pursue unity by following your local church's elder-led practice—perhaps discuss this with them this Sunday to discern how it builds up the body.
What is the Remnant Fellowship?

Remnant Fellowship is a religious organization founded in 1999 by Gwen Shamblin Lara in Brentwood, Tennessee, evolving from her Weigh Down Workshop—a program blending weight loss with a selective interpretation of Scripture that emphasizes obedience to God for personal transformation.

While it claims to represent a biblical "remnant" of true believers (echoing passages like Romans 11:5, where God preserves a faithful remnant by grace), the group departs from core Christian orthodoxy by denying the doctrine of the Trinity—one God eternally existing in three coequal persons, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit—as taught plainly in Matthew 28:19 and 2 Corinthians 13:14. This places it outside historic Christianity, akin to other groups that redefine Christ's deity. If you're exploring faith communities, I encourage testing teachings against the full counsel of Scripture (Acts 17:11) and connecting with a local church rooted in these essentials.
Who wrote the book of 2 John? Who was the author of 2 John?

The Apostle John wrote the book of 2 John, as identified by the author himself as "the elder" (2 John 1:1, Berean Standard Bible). This short epistle, like 1 John and the Gospel of John, bears his distinctive style, vocabulary, and themes of truth, love, and warnings against false teachers, with early church fathers such as Irenaeus and Tertullian unanimously affirming his authorship around AD 85-95.
What is false guilt, and how can I avoid it?

False guilt is the lingering sense of shame or condemnation you feel for sins God has already forgiven through Christ's atonement, or for matters that are not sinful but stem from unrealistic expectations, perfectionism, or Satan's accusations. True guilt, by contrast, is the Holy Spirit's conviction over actual, unconfessed sin, prompting repentance (John 16:8).

Scripture assures believers that "there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1), and when we confess, God is "faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness" (1 John 1:9). He removes our transgressions "as far as the east is from the west" (Psalm 103:12) and promises, "Their sins and their lawless acts I will remember no more" (Hebrews 10:17). False guilt often arises from forgetting our justification by faith alone— Christ's perfect righteousness covers us fully (2 Corinthians 5:21).

To avoid it, rest in the Gospel's finished work: regularly confess known sins, reject accusatory thoughts by affirming God's promises aloud, renew your mind through Scripture meditation, and seek accountability in a local church fellowship where grace is proclaimed. A practical next step: each morning, write down one truth like Romans 8:1 on a card, read it prayerfully, and thank God for your standing in Christ before facing the day. If guilt persists intensely, talk with a pastor or biblical counselor for personalized shepherding.
Are baptism and communion means of grace?

Yes, in the historic Christian tradition, baptism and the Lord's Supper (also called Communion or the Eucharist) are regarded as means of grace—ordinary channels appointed by Christ through which God strengthens and nourishes the faith of believers who receive them worthily.

Among faithful Christians, views differ charitably on the precise nature:
- In Reformed Baptist teaching (as in the 1689 London Baptist Confession), these are ordinances rather than sacraments strictly speaking: visible signs and seals of inward spiritual realities already received by faith. Baptism signifies union with Christ's death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4), while the Lord's Supper proclaims His body broken and blood shed until He returns (1 Corinthians 11:23-26; Luke 22:19-20). God uses them powerfully to confirm grace, stir assurance, and deepen communion with Christ for those who partake by faith, but they do not convey saving grace automatically or apart from the Spirit's work.
- Other orthodox traditions, such as Presbyterian or Lutheran, affirm them more robustly as sacraments that convey grace to worthy recipients (often including infants for baptism), emphasizing God's promise tied to the elements.

Regardless of the emphasis, Scripture calls believers to observe them obediently as acts of remembrance, profession, and joyful dependence on Christ (Matthew 28:19; Acts 2:38; 1 Corinthians 11:26). If you're pondering participation, prayerfully examine your heart and discuss with your local church elders for biblical guidance.
What is the spirit of the law?

The spirit of the law refers to the inner purpose, heart, and transformative intent of God's commandments, which aim to cultivate love for God and neighbor rather than mere external compliance (known as the letter of the law). The Apostle Paul contrasts this in 2 Corinthians 3:6, explaining, "who has made us competent as servants of a new covenant—not of the letter, but of the Spirit; for the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life" (Berean Standard Bible). 

This principle echoes Jesus' teaching in the Sermon on the Mount, where He deepens commands like "do not murder" to include guarding against anger and reconciliation (Matthew 5:21-24), and "do not commit adultery" to embracing purity of heart (Matthew 5:27-28). Ultimately, the spirit of the law is fulfilled when we love God fully and others as ourselves (Romans 13:8-10; Galatians 5:14), empowered by the Holy Spirit in the new covenant (Romans 7:6; Ezekiel 36:26-27). 

For practical application, examine one commandment daily—such as honoring your parents (Exodus 20:12)—and ask how loving God and others through it might shape your actions today, perhaps by forgiving a family tension with grace.
Should pastors be paid a salary?

Yes, the Bible clearly teaches that pastors, described as elders who devote themselves to preaching and teaching, should receive a salary or financial support from the church they serve.

The Apostle Paul instructs in 1 Timothy 5:17-18: "Elders who lead effectively are worthy of double honor, especially those who work hard at preaching and teaching. For the Scripture says, 'Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain,' and, 'The worker is worthy of his wages.'" Similarly, in 1 Corinthians 9:9-14, Paul defends his right to material support by comparing gospel ministry to temple priests and Old Testament farmers, concluding, "In the same way, the Lord has prescribed that those who preach the gospel should receive their living from the gospel." This reflects God's design for those laboring full-time in spiritual oversight to be freed from secular work, ensuring the church benefits from their undivided focus (as seen in Paul's tentmaking exception in Acts 18:3).

Churches honor this by providing fair, modest compensation—often including housing or benefits—tied to the cost of living and family needs, while pastors model sacrificial simplicity. If you're a church leader or member considering this, prayerfully review your elder's needs alongside Galatians 6:6 ("The one who is taught the word is obligated to share all good things with the one who teaches him") and seek wisdom from your congregation's elders.
What does it mean that man shall not live by bread alone (Deuteronomy 8:3)?

Deuteronomy 8:3 teaches that true life depends not merely on physical food like bread, but ultimately on every word that comes from the mouth of the Lord. God humbled Israel in the wilderness by providing manna—a food unknown to them—so they would learn to rely on His sustaining provision and commands rather than self-sufficiency (Deuteronomy 8:3, Berean Standard Bible).

Jesus quotes this verse during His temptation to affirm that spiritual nourishment from Scripture sustains us against sin and trial, prioritizing God's word over bodily needs (Matthew 4:4). In practice, this calls us to daily feed on the Bible through reading, meditation, and obedience, trusting God for both body and soul as our ultimate source of life.
What does the Bible say about parenting?

The Bible portrays parenting as a sacred calling from God, entrusting parents with the stewardship of children as His heritage, to be raised in the discipline and instruction of the Lord for His glory (Psalm 127:3-5; Ephesians 6:4).

Key principles include:

**Love God wholeheartedly and teach your children to do the same.** Parents must model and diligently instill God's commands in daily life: "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is One. Love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength. These words I am commanding you today are to be upon your hearts. And you shall teach them diligently to your children and speak of them when you sit at home and when you walk along the road, when you lie down and when you rise up" (Deuteronomy 6:4-7).

**Provide loving discipline without provocation.** Train children in righteousness to prepare them for life: "Train up a child in the way he should go, and when he is old he will not depart from it" (Proverbs 22:6). Fathers especially are warned, "Do not provoke your children to anger, but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4), and "Fathers, do not embitter your children, or they will become discouraged" (Colossians 3:21).

**Foster obedience and honor within the home.** Children are to obey and honor parents as the first commandment with a promise: "Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right. 'Honor your father and mother' (which is the first commandment with a promise), 'that it may go well with you and that you may have a long life on the earth'" (Ephesians 6:1-3).

Jesus affirmed children's value, welcoming them and saying, "Let the little children come to Me, and do not hinder them! For the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these" (Matthew 19:14).

In practice, this means prioritizing family worship, consistent correction rooted in grace, and pointing children to Christ’s gospel of forgiveness and new life through faith. A concrete next step: Begin nightly family devotions this week, reading one verse like Ephesians 6:1-4 together, discussing it, and praying for one another—trusting God to bless your faithful efforts as He perfects His work in you (Philippians 1:6).
Who was Jeremiah in the Bible?

Jeremiah was a major prophet in the Old Testament, called by God as a young man from the priestly town of Anathoth to warn the southern kingdom of Judah of impending judgment for their idolatry and unfaithfulness (Jeremiah 1:1-5). The son of Hilkiah the priest, he ministered for over 40 years, from the reign of King Josiah around 627 BC until after the fall of Jerusalem to Babylon in 586 BC, earning the title "the weeping prophet" for his deep sorrow over his people's rebellion (Jeremiah 9:1). 

His prophecies, recorded in the Book of Jeremiah, emphasize God's holiness and justice alongside calls to repentance, promises of future restoration, and the announcement of a new covenant written on the heart (Jeremiah 31:31-34). He also authored Lamentations, a poetic outpouring of grief over Jerusalem's destruction. Jeremiah's faithful obedience amid rejection models endurance in proclaiming God's word, reminding us that true prophecy points to Christ's ultimate redemption.
Why did Jesus allow the demons to enter the herd of pigs?

Jesus permitted the demons to enter the herd of pigs because they begged Him not to command them to depart into the abyss, instead pleading to go into the nearby swine, and He gave them leave (Mark 5:10-13; Luke 8:31-32). This act vividly demonstrated Christ's supreme authority over demonic powers, as the unclean spirits obeyed Him instantly, while the pigs—about two thousand in number—rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned (Mark 5:13).

The event underscores several truths: the destructive nature of evil, which ruins both human lives and even animals; the incomparable value Jesus places on people made in God's image, prioritizing the tormented man's deliverance over the economic loss to the pig farmers (Matthew 8:32); and Christ's power to liberate captives from spiritual bondage, foreshadowing His victory over sin and death through His own death and resurrection. As the healed man proclaimed throughout the Decapolis "how much Jesus had done for him," it became a testimony sparking curiosity about the Savior (Mark 5:20).

Today, this reminds us that Jesus remains sovereign over every dark force; if you're facing oppression or fear, cry out to Him in faith, meditate on Mark 5, and seek fellowship with believers who can pray with you.
What are the Analects of Confucius?

The Analects of Confucius, known in Chinese as the *Lunyu* (literally "Selected Sayings"), is a foundational text in Confucianism consisting of brief dialogues, anecdotes, and sayings attributed to the philosopher Confucius (551–479 BC) and his disciples, compiled posthumously between the 5th and 2nd centuries BC.

Divided into 20 books or chapters, it covers topics like ethics, proper conduct, family relationships (especially filial piety), education, governance, and self-cultivation, influencing East Asian thought, politics, and culture for over two millennia.
Can faith change God's plan?

No, faith cannot change God's sovereign plan, for He is immutable and His purposes are eternally fixed and unalterable. As Scripture declares, "I make known the end from the beginning, from ancient times, what is still to come. No matter what happens, I do whatever I please. The One who calls you is faithful, and He will do it" (Isaiah 46:10; 1 Thessalonians 5:24); and "In Him we have obtained an inheritance, having been foreordained according to the purpose of Him who works all things according to the counsel of His will" (Ephesians 1:11).

Faith, rather, is the God-ordained means by which we enter and walk in alignment with His unchanging decree—He sovereignly grants it to accomplish what He has purposed (Ephesians 2:8-10). This truth brings profound comfort: your faithful obedience participates in the very plan God has wisely set from eternity, securing His good outcomes despite our frailty.

If you're wrestling with a specific situation, consider meditating on Psalm 33:11—"The counsel of the LORD stands forever, the purposes of His heart to all generations"—and pray for eyes to see His providence at work, perhaps journaling how past faithfulness has aligned with His faithfulness. What circumstance prompts this question?
What is Pneumatology?

Pneumatology is the branch of Christian theology devoted to the study of the Holy Spirit, the third person of the Trinity—one God eternally existing as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19).

Scripture reveals the Holy Spirit's personhood, deity, and diverse works, such as convicting the world of sin (John 16:8), regenerating believers (John 3:5-6; Titus 3:5), indwelling and empowering them for holiness and witness (Romans 8:9-11; Acts 1:8), and guiding into all truth (John 14:26; John 16:13). This doctrine anchors our understanding of God's active presence in salvation, sanctification, and the life of the church.
What does the Bible say about children's church?

The Bible does not explicitly mention "children's church" as a modern practice of age-segregated children's ministries during corporate worship. Instead, it emphasizes the inclusion of children in the covenant community of God's people for teaching, worship, and instruction in righteousness, while placing primary responsibility on parents for their spiritual nurture.

Scripture repeatedly calls for children to be present with families in gathered worship and learning from God's Word, as seen when all Israel—from infants to the elderly—assembled to hear the Law read aloud (Deuteronomy 31:12; Nehemiah 8:2-3; Joel 2:16). Parents are commanded to diligently teach God's commandments to their children in everyday life, talking about them at home and on the way (Deuteronomy 6:6-7), modeling generational faithfulness so truth endures (Psalm 78:4-7). In the New Testament, children are welcomed in Jesus' presence (Mark 10:13-16), fathers are urged not to provoke their children but to bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord (Ephesians 6:4), and Timothy was discipled from childhood through Scripture (2 Timothy 3:15).

Faithful Christians hold differing views on children's involvement in church as a tertiary matter where Scripture allows liberty (Romans 14:1-6). Some practice family-integrated worship, keeping all ages together to foster intergenerational discipleship and obedience to commands like those in Deuteronomy. Others support age-graded children's ministries alongside family worship, aiming for age-appropriate teaching while parents lead at home, seeing this as a faithful application in diverse church contexts. Both approaches can honor God when rooted in love for His Word and the next generation.

Consider discussing this with your church elders and committing to daily family Bible time, perhaps starting with a simple routine of reading Psalm 78 together and praying for your children's hearts.
Why is the humanity of Jesus important?

Jesus' full humanity—His true incarnation as vera homo, taking on genuine human nature without sin—is essential to the Christian faith because only as man could He perfectly represent us, fulfill God's law on our behalf, and offer Himself as the substitutionary sacrifice for human sin. The Bible teaches that He had to become like His brothers in every way, sharing in flesh and blood, so that He might become a merciful high priest who sympathizes with our weaknesses and makes propitiation for our sins (Hebrews 2:14-17; Hebrews 4:15). Born of a woman under the law, Jesus redeemed those under its curse by living the obedient life we could not and dying the death we deserved (Galatians 4:4-5).

This hypostatic union of divine and human natures in one person secures our salvation: without His humanity, there could be no atoning death for humanity's guilt, no perfect righteousness imputed to believers through justification by faith, and no risen Lord who conquered death in a real human body (1 Timothy 2:5; Romans 5:19). As your shepherding Savior, Jesus knows your temptations and trials intimately—draw near to Him confidently today in prayer, confessing any burdens, for He is able to help you endure (Hebrews 4:16).
What is the new birth?

The new birth, also called regeneration, is the sovereign work of the Holy Spirit in which God imparts new spiritual life to a person dead in sin, transforming their heart from stone to flesh and enabling faith in Christ (Ezekiel 36:26-27; Titus 3:5). Jesus taught that it is essential for entering the kingdom of God, declaring to Nicodemus, "Truly, truly, I tell you, unless a man is born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God" (John 3:3; see also John 3:5-8).

This miracle is not a human achievement but God's gracious initiative, distinct from and preceding justifying faith, as the spiritually dead cannot respond to God on their own (Ephesians 2:1-5). If you sense a longing for this new life, prayerfully read John 3 and ask God to reveal whether He has made you alive in Christ, then seek fellowship with a local church to grow in assurance.
What does the Bible say about education?

The Bible strongly affirms education as a vital responsibility, especially the diligent teaching of God's truth, wisdom, and righteous living to children and believers alike. Parents bear primary accountability to instruct their children in the fear of the Lord, which is declared the beginning of knowledge: "These words that I am commanding you today shall be on your heart. And you shall teach them diligently to your children and speak of them when you sit in your house and when you walk along the road, when you lie down and when you get up" (Deuteronomy 6:6-7, Berean Standard Bible).

Scripture repeatedly urges training in wisdom and godliness: "Train up a child in the way he should go, and when he is old he will not depart from it" (Proverbs 22:6); fathers are commanded, "Do not provoke your children to anger, but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4). All Scripture equips believers for every good work through teaching, reproof, correction, and training in righteousness (2 Timothy 3:16-17). Jesus modeled perfect teaching as Rabbi, calling disciples to learn from Him (Matthew 11:29), while believers are exhorted to "study to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who does not need to be ashamed, correctly handling the word of truth" (2 Timothy 2:15).

While methods of education vary among faithful Christians—public schools, homeschooling, or Christian institutions—the unchanging priority is grounding all learning in God's Word for heart transformation, not mere knowledge. A practical next step: Begin or renew daily family Bible reading and discussion, perhaps using Proverbs for wisdom or the Gospels for Christ's example, to apply these truths in your home.
What is the Edenic covenant?

The Edenic Covenant, also known as the Covenant of Works, is the gracious pre-fall arrangement God established with Adam as humanity's federal head in the Garden of Eden, promising blessing and eternal life for perfect obedience to God's command and the curse of death for disobedience (Genesis 2:16-17). In this covenant, God placed Adam under a probationary test not to eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, with the tree of life symbolizing the reward of confirmed holiness (Genesis 2:9,15-17). This covenant undergirds biblical covenant theology, highlighting human responsibility and the need for a perfect mediator, fulfilled in Christ as the last Adam who succeeded where the first failed (Romans 5:12-21; 1 Corinthians 15:22,45).
What is Wahhabism? What is Wahhabi Islam?

Wahhabism, also called Wahhabi Islam, is a strict revivalist and puritanical movement within Sunni Islam founded by the theologian Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (1703–1792) in Najd, central Arabia. It emphasizes absolute tawhid (the oneness and unity of Allah), condemns shirk (associating partners with God) and bid'ah (religious innovations), and calls for returning to the practices of the salaf (the first three generations of Muslims) by rejecting saint veneration, tomb pilgrimages, and Sufi mysticism.

This movement allied with the Al Saud family in the 18th century, forming the basis of the first Saudi state and profoundly shaping modern Saudi Arabia's religious and political identity, as well as influencing global Salafism. From a Christian perspective, while we appreciate any zeal for the worship of the one true God, Wahhabism rejects core biblical truths such as the Trinity—one God eternally existing in three persons, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14)—and the deity of Jesus Christ, whom Scripture affirms as the eternal Son of God incarnate (John 1:1, 14). If you're exploring faith or worldviews, the Bible invites all to know God through His Son, Jesus Christ (John 14:6).
What is the Watchtower Bible and Tract Society?

The Watchtower Bible and Tract Society is the primary legal and publishing corporation of Jehovah's Witnesses, founded in 1881 by Charles Taze Russell in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, initially as Zion's Watch Tower Tract Society, and now headquartered in Warwick, New York. It oversees the global operations, literature distribution (such as The Watchtower and Awake! magazines), and doctrinal teachings of Jehovah's Witnesses, who number over 8 million active members worldwide.

From a historic Christian perspective, Jehovah's Witnesses' teachings—published through the Watchtower—depart from core biblical doctrines like the Trinity (one God in three persons; Matthew 28:19) and the deity of Christ (John 1:1, 14), placing them outside orthodox Christianity as defined by early creeds and Scripture. If you're exploring their beliefs, I encourage comparing them directly with the Bible alongside a local church pastor for clarity and discernment.
What sort of interfaith ministries are appropriate?

Interfaith ministries can be a fruitful way for Christians to love our neighbors across beliefs, as Jesus commands in Mark 12:31, while upholding the exclusive truth that salvation is through Christ alone, as He declares in John 14:6: "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me." You may wonder if such efforts risk blurring gospel clarity or implying all faiths are equal paths to God—a valid concern, since Scripture calls us to speak truth in love (Ephesians 4:15) without compromise.

Appropriate interfaith ministries focus on practical service and respectful witness, never joint worship or endorsement of other religions as salvific:

- **Humanitarian aid and community service:** Partnering in disaster relief, poverty alleviation, or refugee support (like food pantries or medical clinics) where Christians serve alongside others without theological statements. This embodies the Good Samaritan's compassion (Luke 10:25-37) and opens doors for personal gospel conversations.
  
- **Respectful dialogue and education:** Hosting forums to discuss shared human concerns (e.g., ethics, justice) while clearly presenting Christ's claims. Think of Paul's approach at Mars Hill, reasoning with Athenians about the unknown God and pointing to Jesus' resurrection (Acts 17:22-31).

- **Evangelism in pluralistic settings:** Initiatives like Alpha courses or seeker-friendly events that invite people of all backgrounds to explore Jesus without syncretism.

Inappropriate are those promoting religious pluralism, such as interfaith prayer services equating Jesus with other figures, or ecumenical rituals that obscure penal substitutionary atonement and justification by faith alone.

For your context, prayerfully discuss ideas with your local church elders—they can help discern alignment with the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19-20). A next step: Read Acts 17 this week, noting how Paul built bridges without diluting truth, then journal one way to serve your community lovingly yet boldly. If you'd like examples from faithful ministries, what specific setting are you considering?
What is presuppositional apologetics?

Presuppositional apologetics is a method of defending the Christian faith that starts with the presupposition—or foundational assumption—that the triune God revealed in Scripture is the necessary precondition for all intelligibility, logic, morality, and knowledge itself. Pioneered by Cornelius Van Til and advanced by thinkers like Greg Bahnsen, it rejects the idea of neutral common ground in reasoning, insisting instead that unbelievers suppress innate knowledge of God in unrighteousness, as Paul teaches in Romans 1:18-21.

Biblically, this approach aligns with the conviction that "in [Christ] are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Colossians 2:3), challenging opposing worldviews through transcendental arguments: for example, asking how atheists can justify unchanging laws of logic in a chance universe without borrowing from the Christian God who is Himself logical and unchanging. In practice, it equips believers to expose inconsistencies in secular thought while pointing to Christ as the only coherent foundation for reality.
What does the Bible say about chanting? Should a Christian chant?

The Bible does not explicitly command or forbid "chanting" as a modern practice, but it distinguishes between vain, repetitive babbling in prayer, which Jesus warns against, and heartfelt, God-centered repetition in worship, as seen in the Psalms. For example, Matthew 6:7 cautions, "And when you are praying, do not babble on like pagans, for they think that by their many words they will be heard," highlighting the emptiness of mechanical or manipulative repetition aimed at earning divine favor.

In contrast, Scripture models joyful, repetitive praise that exalts God's character, such as Psalm 136, where the congregation responsively declares twenty-six times, "His loving devotion endures forever," or the unified singing in 2 Chronicles 5:13, "For He is good; for His loving devotion endures forever." The New Testament further encourages believers to address "one another with psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, singing and making music in your hearts to the Lord" (Ephesians 5:19), emphasizing truth-saturated, Spirit-led worship over form.

A Christian should avoid chanting that mimics pagan rituals or relies on empty repetition divorced from Scripture and the heart (Colossians 3:16), as this risks idolatry or superstition. However, incorporating biblical repetition—like meditating on God's attributes through responsive psalms—can enrich worship when it flows from genuine faith and glorifies Christ. Faithful Christians differ on worship styles here, with some embracing structured chants in liturgy and others preferring free-form singing; the priority is always a heart attuned to God (John 4:24).

To apply this, examine your motive: begin by reading Psalm 136 aloud responsively in personal or family worship, letting God's enduring love reshape your praise.
Who is Jesus — Day 4 — Jesus the Prophet

Jesus Christ is the promised Prophet greater than Moses, the ultimate revelation of God to humanity, who speaks the Father's words with perfect authority and truth.

In the Old Testament, God promised through Moses, "The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers; you shall listen to him" (Deuteronomy 18:15). This Prophet would declare God's words exactly as received, holding people accountable to heed Him or face judgment (Deuteronomy 18:18-19). Jesus fulfilled this perfectly, as Peter proclaimed after Pentecost: "For Moses said, 'The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers. You must listen to Him in all that He tells you'" (Acts 3:22). Unlike partial revelations through the prophets of old, God has now spoken fully and finally through His Son (Hebrews 1:1-2).

Jesus lived as Prophet in His earthly ministry, teaching with unmatched authority—"for He was teaching them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes" (Matthew 7:29)—and revealing the Father intimately: "No one has ever seen God; the only God, who is at the Father's side, He has made Him known" (John 1:18). His miracles confirmed His words as divine truth (John 10:37-38), and even demons submitted to His prophetic command (Mark 1:27).

As your shepherding guide, consider this: Jesus as Prophet calls you to listen obediently today. Take an actionable step—read one chapter from the Gospel of John this week, noting how Jesus reveals God's heart, then pray, asking the Holy Spirit to help you heed His words amid life's noise. What specific teaching of Jesus is the Spirit pressing on your heart right now?
Will there be a second chance for salvation after death?

No, the Bible teaches there is no second chance for salvation after death. As Hebrews 9:27 states, "And just as it is appointed for mortals to die once, and after that, to face judgment" (Berean Standard Bible). Death marks the end of the opportunity for repentance and faith; what we do with Jesus Christ in this life determines our eternal destiny.

You may wonder about this because God's mercy seems so vast, or perhaps you're thinking of loved ones who have passed away without faith in Christ—those are honest and common concerns. Yet Scripture is clear: salvation comes only through faith in Christ's atoning death and resurrection during our earthly lives. God is holy and just, and sin separates us from Him; humanity's rebellion deserves wrath, but Jesus, fully God and fully man, lived perfectly, died substitutionarily on the cross in our place, was buried, rose bodily on the third day, and now reigns as Lord. He alone reconciles sinners to God by grace through faith alone, not works (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 10:9).

The time for decision is now, as 2 Corinthians 6:2 declares: "For He says: 'In the time of favor I heard you, and in the day of salvation I helped you.' Behold, now is the time of favor; now is the day of salvation!" Turn from sin, trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you, and receive forgiveness and eternal life today. If you're ready, cry out to God in prayer right now, confessing your need and believing His promises—then find a faithful local church to grow in the faith. What is holding you back from trusting Him fully?
What is biblical homemaking?
